I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.
Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?

,

To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.
Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.
,Both Squeak and Pharo are popular implementations of Smalltalk and are suitable for beginners. However, there are some differences between the two that may help you decide which one to start with.

Squeak is one of the oldest Smalltalk implementations and is known for its simplicity and ease of use. It has a large community and a wealth of resources available for beginners. Squeak is also highly customizable, allowing users to easily modify and extend the system to suit their needs. However, some users may find the user interface of Squeak to be outdated compared to modern software.

On the other hand, Pharo is a more modern implementation of Smalltalk that aims to improve upon the shortcomings of Squeak. It has a cleaner and more modern user interface, making it more appealing to users who are used to working with contemporary software. Pharo also has a strong emphasis on tools and development environments, making it a good choice for those who are looking to dive deeper into Smalltalk development.

Ultimately, the choice between Squeak and Pharo comes down to personal preference and the specific needs of the user. If you prefer a simpler and more traditional Smalltalk experience, Squeak may be the better choice. If you are looking for a more modern and feature-rich implementation, Pharo may be more suitable.

It is recommended to try out both implementations and see which one you feel more comfortable with. Additionally, reaching out to the Smalltalk community for advice and guidance can also help you make an informed decision.",252,,,,,0.0,0.5139618144556178,0,[],[],154,1438,306,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

One possible regex pattern that you can use to match all words in a phrase is `\b\w+\b`. Here's what each part of the pattern does:

So, your updated regex pattern would be `\b\w+\b`. This will match all words in a phrase.

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

• We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
• We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
• You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.

,In Pharo, the use of `self subclassResponsibility` in a method indicates that the responsibility for implementing that method lies with the subclasses of the current class. This is a way of enforcing that subclasses must provide their own implementation of the method, rather than relying on the superclass's implementation.

In the case of the `size` method in `AbstractArrayDictionary`, using `self subclassResponsibility` ensures that any subclass of `AbstractArrayDictionary` must implement their own version of the `size` method. This is important because the implementation of `size` may vary depending on the specific subclass. For example, a `TreeBasedDictionary` may calculate size differently than a `SimpleDictionary`.

If you were to implement `size` in `AbstractDictionary` as `^self size`, it would defeat the purpose of having subclasses provide their own implementation. This would mean that all subclasses would inherit the same implementation from `AbstractDictionary`, which may not be appropriate for all subclasses.

Therefore, by using `self subclassResponsibility`, you are enforcing that each subclass must provide its own implementation of the `size` method, ensuring that the behavior is tailored to the specific subclass.

As for removing the method from `AbstractArrayDictionary`, it would depend on whether you want to enforce the implementation of `size` in all subclasses of `AbstractArrayDictionary`. If you remove the method from `AbstractArrayDictionary`, subclasses would not be required to implement their own version of `size`, which may or may not be desirable depending on your design goals.",228,,,,,0.0,0.5960776978960258,0,[],[],601,990,309,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

,Smalltalk is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"" because the type of a variable is determined at runtime based on the value it holds. In Smalltalk, variables do not have explicit type annotations like in statically typed languages such as Java or C++. Instead, the type of a variable is inferred based on the object it references. For example, if a variable x is assigned an integer value, then x is considered to be of type Integer.

While Smalltalk does not have explicit type annotations, it does have a strong type system where objects are classified based on their class hierarchy. Each object in Smalltalk belongs to a specific class, and the class hierarchy allows for dynamic method dispatch based on the actual type of the object at runtime.

In contrast, ""untyped"" languages typically do not have any concept of types at all, meaning that variables can hold any type of value without restriction. Smalltalk, on the other hand, does have types in the form of classes, but these types are determined dynamically at runtime.

Therefore, Smalltalk is considered dynamically typed because the type of a variable is determined dynamically based on the value it holds, as opposed to being explicitly declared in the code. This allows for flexibility and dynamic behavior in the language.

GenAI,"Why do methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk?","

Background
In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or ""self"" in the message context).

column3: is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

column1: • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?

,

One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows ""self"". By returning ""self"" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the ""self"" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning ""self"" is the most elegant thing to do.
,In Smalltalk, methods return ""self"" by default because of the language's emphasis on message passing and object-oriented programming principles.

The idea behind this design is to promote a clear and consistent way of interacting with objects. By default, if a method does not explicitly return anything, the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or ""self"" in the message context). This means that the object that received the message is returned, allowing for a more intuitive and predictable behavior.

The philosophical background behind this design is rooted in the concept of encapsulation and object-oriented design. By returning ""self"" by default, Smalltalk encourages a more cohesive and self-contained approach to programming, where objects are responsible for managing their own state and behavior.

One practical benefit of this design is that it can facilitate method chaining, where multiple methods can be called in succession on the same object. This can lead to more concise and readable code, as well as a more fluent and expressive programming style.

Overall, the default return of ""self"" in Smalltalk methods aligns with the language's object-oriented principles and helps to promote a more cohesive and intuitive approach to programming.",188,,,,,0.1666666666666666,0.6181980750250287,1,['Important By default ( if not specified differently ) a method returns the message receiver .'],"['By default , if a method does not explicitly return anything , the message passing evaluates to the receiver ( or "" self "" in the message context ) .']",259,1345,230,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

,

The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.

The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.
Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
which is a little bit more verbose.
To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.

,In Smalltalk, the terms ""self"" and ""yourself"" have distinct meanings. The keyword ""self"" represents the receiver of the message within a method (Pharo By Example, p. 103). It is similar to the keyword ""this"" in Java and is used to indicate that the method is returning the object that received the message. When a method does not have an explicit return statement, it is assumed to end with ""^ self"" (Pharo By Example, p. 103).

On the other hand, ""yourself"" is not a keyword in Smalltalk but is often used in method chains to refer to the result of the previous message send (Pharo By Example, p. 103). It is a way to continue a series of messages on the result of the previous message.

Additionally, it is important to note that ""self"" and ""super"" have different roles in Smalltalk. While ""self"" represents the receiver of the message and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver, ""super"" represents the receiver of the message, but the method lookup starts in the superclass of the class where the super send occurs (Pharo By Example, p. 105).

In summary, ""self"" is used to refer to the object inside which the current method is executing, while ""yourself"" is not a keyword but is commonly used in method chains. RAG,Smalltalk initialization variables,"

In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.
How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?
Or is this bad practice and should I always do

,

The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

• We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
• We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
• You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.
There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

[*] This is quite natural due to the orthogonal nature on Smalltalk, since everything (including classes) is an object. If you are interested check Chapter 13 of Pharo by Example or any other reference to classes and metaclasses in Smalltalk.

,In Pharo Smalltalk, initialization variables can be handled in various ways. One approach is lazy initialization, where variables are initialized only when needed, such as when they are expensive to initialize or when users don't typically expect them to be initialized immediately (Pharo By Example, p. 96).

For class variables like those in the Color class, one way to initialize them is through an accessor method that initializes the variable if it has not been already (Pharo By Example, p. 111). This method can be called when needed to ensure the variable is properly initialized.

Alternatively, you can define class methods within a class to handle initialization tasks. For example, in the case of a Dog class, you can define class methods to initialize a count variable to 0 and increment it when new instances are created (Pharo By Example, p. 111). This allows for more control over the initialization process and ensures that variables are set up correctly.

Overall, while there are different ways to handle initialization variables in Pharo Smalltalk, it is important to consider factors such as when variables need to be initialized, how they will be accessed, and whether lazy initialization or explicit initialization methods are more appropriate for the specific use case.",205,,,,,0.375,0.6564103539957541,3,"['Similarly , if a variable is expensive to initialize ( opening a database connection or a network socket , for example ) , you will sometimes choose to delay that initialization until you actually need it .', 'This can be done by introducing an accessor method which , when executed , initializes the variable if it has not yet been initialized .', ', and to increment it when new instances are created : Dog class > > new count : = count +1 .']","[""One approach is lazy initialization , where variables are initialized only when needed , such as when they are expensive to initialize or when users do n't typically expect them to be initialized immediately ( Pharo By Example , p. 96 ) ."", 'For example , in the case of a Dog class , you can define class methods to initialize a count variable to 0 and increment it when new instances are created ( Pharo By Example , p. 111 ) .', 'For class variables like those in the Color class , one way to initialize them is through an accessor method that initializes the variable if it has not been already ( Pharo By Example , p. 111 ) .']",179,1409,248,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

1. The method selector doesn't start with a lowercase letter
It is a tradition for selectors to start with a lowercase letter. In this sense, genLista would be more correct than GenLista.
2. The method selector includes the abbreviated word 'gen'
For instance, genLista could be renamed to genereLista o listaAlAzar (if you decide to use Spanish)
The result of Array new: 31 is an array of 31 elements. However, the code below it only fills 30 of them, leaving the last one uninitialized (i.e., nil). Possible solution: lista := Array new: 30.
4. A dot is missing causing a compilation error
<pre><code>  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]

does not compile because there is no dot indicating the separation between the two sentences. Note that the error happens at compilation time (i.e., when you save the method) because the return token ^ must start a statement (i.e., it cannot be inlined inside a statement).
There are other cases where a missing dot will not prevent the code from compiling. Instead, an error will happen at runtime. Here is a (typical) example:
the missing dot will generate the runtime error self not understood by block.
5. There is a more expressive way of generating 0s and 1s at random
The calculation 2 atRandom - 1 is ok. However, it forces the reader to mentally do the math. A better way to reveal your intention would have been
6. When playing with random numbers don't forget to save the seed

While it is ok to use atRandom, such a practice should only be used with ""toy"" code. If you are developing a system or a library, the recommended practice is to save the seed somewhere before generating any random data. This will allow you to reproduce the generation of random quantities later on for the sake of debugging or confirmation. (Note however, that this will not suffice for making your program deterministically reproducible because unordered (e.g. hashed) collections could form differently in successive executions.)
,To create an array with random numbers (either 0 or 1) in Pharo Smalltalk, you can use the following code snippet (Pharo By Example, p. 203):
1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: (2 atRandom - 1)]

In this code, the `do:` method is used to iterate over the indices of the array from 1 to 30. For each index `i`, a random number between 0 and 1 is generated using the expression `(2 atRandom - 1)`. This ensures that the numbers inserted into the array are either 0 or 1.

It's important to note that in Pharo, arrays are indexed starting from 1, not 0 (Pharo By Example, p. 203). The `at:` method is used to access elements in the array by index, and `at:put:` is used to insert elements into the array.

Additionally, it's recommended not to modify literal arrays directly, as they are stored in compiled method literal frames. If you need to modify an array, it's best to create a copy of it to avoid unexpected behavior (Pharo By Example, p. 204).

,In Pharo, the usage of `self subclassResponsibility` is a way to indicate that a method should be implemented by subclasses of a particular class. This is commonly used for abstract methods in abstract classes, which are classes meant to be subclassed rather than instantiated directly (Pharo By Example, p. 97). By convention, the body of an abstract method consists of the expression `self subclassResponsibility`, signaling that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method (Pharo By Example, p. 97).

When a method in a superclass sends `self subclassResponsibility`, it serves as a framework for the behavior of the class's subclasses, indicating that the subclass should have implemented this message (Pharo By Example, p. 97). This approach helps in enforcing the implementation of specific methods in subclasses, ensuring that the necessary functionality is provided at the appropriate level of the inheritance hierarchy.

For example, if a class like `AbstractArrayDictionary` has a method `size` implemented as `self subclassResponsibility`, it means that subclasses of `AbstractArrayDictionary` should override and provide their own implementation of the `size` method (Pharo By Example, p. 97). This approach encourages proper subclassing behavior and ensures that each subclass provides its own implementation of essential methods.

In contrast, using `^self size` in the `size` method would not enforce the implementation of `size` in subclasses, potentially leading to inconsistencies or missing functionality in subclasses (Pharo By Example, p. 97). By using `self subclassResponsibility`, the responsibility is explicitly placed on subclasses to provide their own implementation, promoting a more structured and consistent inheritance hierarchy.

Therefore, when implementing methods like `size` in superclass and subclass relationships, using `self subclassResponsibility` is a recommended practice to enforce the implementation of essential methods in subclasses and maintain a clear and consistent inheritance structure (Pharo By Example, p. 97).",292,,,,,0.2,0.6621743576697049,2,"['Instead , by convention , the body of an abstract method consists of the expression self subclassResponsibility .', 'Here is a typical application : subclassResponsibility "" This message sets up a framework for the behavior of the class \' subclasses .""']","['By convention , the body of an abstract method consists of the expression ` self subclassResponsibility ` , signaling that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method ( Pharo By Example , p. 97 ) .', ""When a method in a superclass sends ` self subclassResponsibility ` , it serves as a framework for the behavior of the class 's subclasses , indicating that the subclass should have implemented this message ( Pharo By Example , p. 97 ) .""]",643,948,396,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

,To create class (static) methods in Pharo 4.0, you need to define them on the class side of the class. For instance, if you have a class called CreateUser with instance variables name, email, and phone, the method declaration should be as follows: `CreateUser class>>createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone` (Pharo By Example, p. 92). To call this static method, you should use the class name followed by the method name and arguments, like this:

However, if you want to create an instance of the class and set its instance variables, you should use the `new` keyword. The method declaration remains the same, but it should be defined on the instance side of the class. When calling the method to create a new instance, you should use the `new` keyword like this:

If you encounter an error message like ""MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >> name:email:phone:"", it means there is a mismatch in the method declaration. Make sure the method is correctly defined on the class side as `CreateUser: name: email: phone:` (Pharo By Example, p. 92).

In Pharo, instance variables can only be accessed within the class that defines them or in its subclasses. Accessor methods need to be defined to access instance variables from other classes (Pharo By Example, p. 89). The concept of static variables/methods in Java is different from class methods in Pharo. In Pharo, class methods are dynamically-dispatched, allowing for inheritance, overriding, and super-sends, unlike static methods in Java (Pharo By Example, p. 92).

To access instance variables in Pharo, you can add accessor methods for class/instance variables and then call them with the class instance or class object instance (Pharo By Example, p. 89). Remember that in Pharo, everything happens by sending messages, and access to instance variables of other objects is restricted unless accessor methods are defined (Pharo By Example, p. 113).",340,,,,,0.3636363636363636,0.6287423218831183,4,"['In Pharo , all instance variables are private to the object that owns them , so the only way for another object to read or write those variables is through accessor methods like this one .', ""In Pharo , two instances of the same class can not access each other 's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods ."", ""This means that inheritance , overriding and super - sends work for class methods in Pharo , whereas they do n't work for static methods in Java ."", 'Java and C++ static variables are more like Pharo class variables ( discussed in Section 6.9 ) , since in those languages all of the subclasses and all of their instances share the same static variable .']","['To access instance variables in Pharo , you can add accessor methods for class / instance variables and then call them with the class instance or class object instance ( Pharo By Example , p. 89 ) .', 'Accessor methods need to be defined to access instance variables from other classes ( Pharo By Example , p. 89 ) .', 'In Pharo , instance variables can only be accessed within the class that defines them or in its subclasses .', 'In Pharo , class methods are dynamically - dispatched , allowing for inheritance , overriding , and super - sends , unlike static methods in Java ( Pharo By Example , p. 92 ) .', 'The concept of static variables / methods in Java is different from class methods in Pharo .', 'Remember that in Pharo , everything happens by sending messages , and access to instance variables of other objects is restricted unless accessor methods are defined ( Pharo By Example , p. 113 ) .']",522,1078,474,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!
I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?

,

Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.
If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the
• „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
• open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.

,If your Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, there are several ways to break out of it. One method is to interrupt the loop and open a debugger on the computation by typing CMD-. This will bring up a pre-debugger window where you can examine the execution and either proceed or make changes to the code (Pharo By Example, p. 140). Another option is to edit the suspect code to insert a halt command, which will bring up the pre-debugger when executed (Pharo By Example, p. 140).

Additionally, you can set breakpoints in your code to halt execution at specific points. However, setting breakpoints in heavily used methods like Morph>>openInWorld can cause issues, as the debugger may be triggered too soon. To conditionally halt only in specific contexts, you can use the haltIf: method, specifying the condition under which the halt should occur (Pharo By Example, pp. 311-312).

In cases where setting breakpoints using self halt may not be feasible, you can use the Object>>haltIf: method to set intelligent breakpoints. This method allows you to halt execution only in specific contexts of interest, preventing the debugger from opening prematurely (Pharo By Example, pp. 311-312). By utilizing these techniques, you can effectively break out of infinite loops and debug your Pharo image without losing your work.",214,,,,,0.6,0.7266736664545347,6,"['You can also just edit the suspect code to insert Halt now .', 'If you execute code that goes into an infinite loop , you can interrupt it and open a debugger on the computation by typing CMD- .', 'You should see a pre - debugger window ( Figure 13 - 6 ) showing a WACounter object executing a halt .', 'You can also use Halt once or Halt if : aCondition .', '* Intelligent breakpoints can be set using haltIf : , taking a method selector as its argument .', 'The problem is clear once we understand that 1 ) Morph>>openInWorld is used by many parts of the system , so the breakpoint is triggered very soon after we interact with the user interface , but 2 ) the debugger itself sends openInWorld as soon as it opens a window , preventing the debugger from opening !']","['Another option is to edit the suspect code to insert a halt command , which will bring up the pre - debugger when executed ( Pharo By Example , p. 140 ) .', 'One method is to interrupt the loop and open a debugger on the computation by typing CMD- .', 'This will bring up a pre - debugger window where you can examine the execution and either proceed or make changes to the code ( Pharo By Example , p. 140 ) .', 'To conditionally halt only in specific contexts , you can use the haltIf : method , specifying the condition under which the halt should occur ( Pharo By Example , pp . 311 - 312 ) .', 'In cases where setting breakpoints using self halt may not be feasible , you can use the Object>>haltIf : method to set intelligent breakpoints .', 'However , setting breakpoints in heavily used methods like Morph>>openInWorld can cause issues , as the debugger may be triggered too soon .']",220,1389,277,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

Every class defines both the shape (or structure) and the behavior of its instances. The shape is given by the instance variables in the class creation message, such as
For the behavior you create methods in the instance side of the class
With this you have a full functional object
However, classes are objects too. So, they may have their own shape. To add an instance variable to a class's shape you send the message:
Note that the receiver of this message is the class of the Dog class (a Metaclass), which makes sense because it is classes who shape their instances.

In your example, every time you create a new (instance of) Dog the count ivar of the Dog class will increase by 1. This could serve the propose of keeping track of how many instances of Dog have been created so far. These instances, however, will remain unaware of this fact.

Yes, the ivar count and the methods to initialize, increment and retrieve it belong in the class side. Why? Because they are intended to keep track of the number of instances your class has created. Whether this is useful or not, is a different question

column1: ,To create a class in Pharo Smalltalk, you can follow the example provided in the textbook ""Pharo By Example"" (p. 119-120). You can start by editing the class creation template in the browser pane, replacing ""Object"" with the name of the existing class you want to subclass and ""NameOfSubclass"" with the desired name for your new subclass. Additionally, you can fill in the instance variable names if known. It is recommended to subclass Object or one of its subclasses when creating application classes unless there is a specific reason not to do so (p. 97).

When creating a new class, you typically send the message subclass: instanceVariableNames: to an existing class. This message creates a new class with the specified instance variables. It is important to note that every class in Pharo has a superclass, and class methods can be defined to initialize variables or perform utility functions (p. 96).

In the example of creating a Dog and Hyena class, you can define class methods for Dog to initialize its count to 0 and increment it when new instances are created. For instance, you can define methods like Dog class >> new and Dog class >> count to manage the count value of the class Dog. These methods can be placed on the class side of the class (p. 93).

When defining methods for a class, it is essential to differentiate between instance side and class side methods. Instance side methods are created on the instance side of the class and are executed when an object is instantiated. On the other hand, class side methods do not require an object to be created and are similar to static methods in Java. It is recommended to put methods related to class behavior on the class side and instance-specific methods on the instance side (p. 91-92).

In conclusion, when creating a class in Pharo Smalltalk, you can follow the class creation template, define class methods for initialization and utility functions, and differentiate between instance side and class side methods based on their intended functionality. ,To automatically generate getter/setter code for a class in Pharo or Squeak, you can dynamically add accessors for instance variables on demand. This can be achieved by creating a method that traps messages not understood, such as doesNotUnderstand: in a class like DynamicAccessors (p. 315). By following the Pharo convention, where getters have the same name as the variable they get and setters have a trailing "":"", you can easily generate accessors for instance variables (p. 49).

To add or modify class definitions dynamically in Smalltalk, you can pass a list of instance variable names to a method that will create them automatically. For example, you can use a method like addVariablesAndAccessors(className: MyClass, variablesList: ('aaaa', 'bbbb', 'cccc')) to generate the instance variables and accessors for a class (p. 315). This approach allows for a pure object-oriented way of working with Smalltalk and dynamically modifying class definitions.

In Pharo, all instance variables are private to the object that owns them, but the inspector can access them without needing accessors using the reflective abilities of Pharo (p. 298). This means that you can dynamically create accessors for instance variables as needed, providing flexibility in modifying class definitions on the fly.

Overall, by leveraging the dynamic nature of Smalltalk and Pharo, you can generate getter/setter code automatically for a class by creating methods that add accessors for instance variables on demand. This approach allows for a more flexible and dynamic way of working with class definitions in Smalltalk.",242,,,,,0.5,0.69441444004817,3,"['Let us look at a simple variant of this technique where we have a class that automatically adds accessors for its instance variables on demand : DynamicAcccessors > > doesNotUnderstand :', 'In practice , the inspector can access instance variables without needing accessors , because it uses the reflective abilities of Pharo .', 'You can define instance variables and methods on the fly .']","['To automatically generate getter / setter code for a class in Pharo or Squeak , you can dynamically add accessors for instance variables on demand .', 'In Pharo , all instance variables are private to the object that owns them , but the inspector can access them without needing accessors using the reflective abilities of Pharo ( p. 298 ) .', 'This means that you can dynamically create accessors for instance variables as needed , providing flexibility in modifying class definitions on the fly .']",373,1237,321,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

RAG,"Why is Smalltalk sometimes called ""dynamically typed""?","

I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"" and not ""untyped"" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.
There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?

,

""Dynamically typed"" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean ""dynamically checked"". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.
See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.

,Smalltalk is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"" because it is a dynamically-typed language, meaning that type checking is done at runtime rather than at compile time (Pharo By Example, p. 1). In Smalltalk, there are no type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns, so the type of an object is determined by its class (Pharo By Example, p. 1). This dynamic typing allows for flexibility in the language and enables objects to respond to messages based on their individual methods, rather than being restricted by a static type system (Pharo By Example, p. 100).

In Smalltalk, nearly everything happens by sending messages, including variable declarations, assignments, and returns (Pharo By Example, pp. 100-101). This message passing model allows for objects to have their own methods for responding to messages, leading to a more flexible and dynamic system (Pharo By Example, p. 100). Additionally, Smalltalk uses dynamically-dispatched methods, which means that inheritance, overriding, and super-sends work for class methods, unlike in languages with static methods like Java (Pharo By Example, pp. 183-184).

Overall, Smalltalk's dynamic typing and message passing model contribute to its reputation as a dynamically-typed language, where types are determined at runtime and objects can respond to messages dynamically based on their individual methods and classes.",205,,,,,0.4285714285714285,0.669582933568111,3,"['Ok , so nearly everything in Pharo happens by sending messages .', ""This means that inheritance , overriding and super - sends work for class methods in Pharo , whereas they do n't work for static methods in Java ."", ""One of the consequences of Pharo 's model of message sending is that it encourages a style in which objects tend to have very small methods and delegate tasks to other objects , rather than implementing huge , procedural methods that assume too much responsibility .""]","['In Smalltalk , nearly everything happens by sending messages , including variable declarations , assignments , and returns ( Pharo By Example , pp .)', 'Additionally , Smalltalk uses dynamically - dispatched methods , which means that inheritance , overriding , and super - sends work for class methods , unlike in languages with static methods like Java ( Pharo By Example , pp .)', 'dynamically - dispatched methods , which means that inheritance , overriding , and super - sends work for class methods , unlike in languages with static methods like Java ( Pharo By Example , pp)', 'This message passing model allows for objects to have their own methods for responding to messages , leading to a more flexible and dynamic system ( Pharo By Example , p. 100 ) .']",224,1377,285,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

Instance variables of the class side. You can define that when you switch to the class side, then you will se a code like this:

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them ""pool variables"", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. ,In Pharo, the difference between ""instance Variable Names"" and ""class Variable Names"" lies in their accessibility and scope. Instance variables are private to the instance itself, meaning that each instance of a class has its own set of instance variables that cannot be accessed directly by other instances of the same class unless accessor methods are defined (Pharo By Example, p. 89). On the other hand, class variables are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself, with the lifetime of the variable being the same as that of the class (Pharo By Example, pp. 109-110).

For instance variables, access is limited to the class that defines them and its subclasses, similar to protected variables in C++ and Java (Pharo By Example, p. 89). In contrast, class variables are accessible by all instances of a class and the class itself, providing a way to share data among them (Pharo By Example, pp. 109-110).

Therefore, while instance variables are private to each instance, class variables are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself, allowing for data sharing and access across multiple objects in Pharo.",188,,,,,0.8333333333333334,0.7525136131635439,5,"['Instance variables Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself .', 'This means that Pharo instance variables are similar to protected variables in C++ and Java .', 'Class variables are private variables shared by the class and all the instances of the class .', 'Class variables are shared between a class , its subclasses and its instances .', '109 The Pharo object model Figure 6 - 27 Instance and class methods accessing different variables Class variables Sometimes we need to share some data amongst all the instances of a class and the class itself .']","['Instance variables are private to the instance itself , meaning that each instance of a class has its own set of instance variables that can not be accessed directly by other instances of the same class unless accessor methods are defined ( Pharo By Example , p. 89 ) .', 'For instance variables , access is limited to the class that defines them and its subclasses , similar to protected variables in C++ and Java ( Pharo By Example , p. 89 ) .', 'Therefore , while instance variables are private to each instance , class variables are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself , allowing for data sharing and access across multiple objects in Pharo .', 'On the other hand , class variables are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself , with the lifetime of the variable being the same as that of the class ( Pharo By Example , pp .)', 'In contrast , class variables are accessible by all instances of a class and the class itself , providing a way to share data among them ( Pharo By Example , pp .)']",192,1413,238,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

RAG,"Why do methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk?","

Background
In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or ""self"" in the message context).

column3: is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

column1: • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?

,

One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows ""self"". By returning ""self"" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the ""self"" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning ""self"" is the most elegant thing to do.

,In Smalltalk, methods return ""self"" by default because the pseudo-variable ""self"" represents the receiver of the message, similar to the keyword ""this"" in Java (Pharo By Example, p. 103). When a method does not explicitly return anything, the message passing evaluates to the receiver, which is ""self"" in the message context (Pharo By Example, p. 103). This design choice encourages a style where objects have small methods and delegate tasks to other objects, promoting good object-oriented practices (Pharo By Example, p. 101).

By default, a method in Smalltalk returns the message receiver if not specified differently (Pharo By Example, p. 104). Explicitly writing ""^ self"" at the end of a method is not recommended unless you intend for the sender to use the value returned (Pharo By Example, p. 103). This approach simplifies method chaining and promotes a clear communication of intent between objects in the system.

Overall, the philosophy behind methods returning ""self"" by default in Smalltalk is to promote encapsulation, delegation, and good object-oriented design practices. This design choice ensures that objects communicate by sending messages and encourages a modular and flexible approach to programming (Pharo By Example, p. 85-87).",190,,,,,0.6666666666666666,0.7188646588416835,4,"['Important By default ( if not specified differently ) a method returns the message receiver .', 'We usually say that the method answers self , because in Pharo the pseudovariable self represents the receiver of the message , much like the keyword this in Java .', 'This expression will trigger the Pharo tutorial ( as shown in Figure 2 - 11 ) .', ""One of the consequences of Pharo 's model of message sending is that it encourages a style in which objects tend to have very small methods and delegate tasks to other objects , rather than implementing huge , procedural methods that assume too much responsibility .""]","['By default , a method in Smalltalk returns the message receiver if not specified differently ( Pharo By Example , p. 104 ) .', 'In Smalltalk , methods return "" self "" by default because the pseudo - variable "" self "" represents the receiver of the message , similar to the keyword "" this "" in Java ( Pharo By Example , p. 103 ) .', 'When a method does not explicitly return anything , the message passing evaluates to the receiver , which is "" self "" in the message context ( Pharo By Example , p. 103 ) .', '( Pharo By Example , p. 85 - 87)', 'This design choice encourages a style where objects have small methods and delegate tasks to other objects , promoting good object - oriented practices ( Pharo By Example , p. 101 ) .', 'This design choice ensures that objects communicate by sending messages and encourages a modular and flexible approach to programming ( Pharo By Example , p. 85 - 87 ) .']",301,1303,261,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

,To check if an element already exists in a set in Pharo, you can use the Set class, which behaves as a mathematical set with no duplicate elements and no specific order (Pharo By Example, p. 207). Elements can be added to a Set using the `add:` message, and you can create sets using `Set newFrom:` or `Collection>>asSet:` (Pharo By Example, p. 207). For example, if you have a set `s` with elements 4/2, 4, and 2, the size of the set would be 2 after adding these elements (Pharo By Example, p. 207).

To eliminate duplicates from a collection, you can use the `asSet` method, which offers a convenient way to remove duplicates (Pharo By Example, p. 207). For instance, if you have a collection of colors and you convert it to a set, the size of the set will reflect the unique elements in the collection (Pharo By Example, p. 207).

If you want to test set membership in Pharo, you can use the `includes:` method, which checks if a specific element is present in the set (Pharo By Example, p. 163). For example, in a test method like `testIncludes`, you can assert that a set includes a certain element (Pharo By Example, p. 163).

In the context of testing, you can also verify occurrences of elements in a set using methods like `occurrencesOf:` (Pharo By Example, p. 163). For instance, in a test method like `testOccurrences`, you can check the number of times a specific element appears in a set (Pharo By Example, p. 163).

To ensure that an element is not added to a set if it already exists, you can combine the `includes:` method with conditional statements to check for the presence of the element before adding it (Pharo By Example, p. 163). This way, you can prevent duplicate elements from being added to the set.

Overall, in Pharo, you can use the Set class and its methods like `add:`, `includes:`, and `asSet` to manage collections and check for the existence of elements in a set effectively.",337,,,,,0.4285714285714285,0.6486660686680954,3,"['In a Set , elements are added using the message add : and they can not be accessed using the message at : .', 'self assert : ( full includes : 6 ) Listing 9 - 4 Testing occurrences MyExampleSetTest > > testOccurrences self assert : ( empty occurrencesOf : 0 )', 'The first test , named testIncludes , tests the includes : method of Set .']","['For example , in a test method like ` testIncludes ` , you can assert that a set includes a certain element ( Pharo By Example , p. 163 ) .', 'In the context of testing , you can also verify occurrences of elements in a set using methods like ` occurrencesOf : ` ( Pharo By Example , p. 163 ) .', 'Elements can be added to a Set using the ` add : ` message , and you can create sets using ` Set newFrom : ` or ` Collection>>asSet : ` ( Pharo By Example , p. 207 ) .']",279,1324,471,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

RAG,Message Not Understood for existing method,"

Ok, so it's like this:
Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?

,

You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.

,When a message is not understood for an existing method, the Pharo system follows a convoluted path to handle the error. If a method is not found in the inheritance chain, the object will send itself the message doesNotUnderstand, triggering the debugger (Pharo By Example, pp. 107-108). This allows developers to intercept errors and provide alternative actions by overriding the Object>>doesNotUnderstand: method in subclasses of Object (Pharo By Example, p. 107).

One way to fix the issue of a message not being understood is to override the doesNotUnderstand: method in a subclass of Object to provide a different way of handling the error (Pharo By Example, pp. 312-313). This can be useful for implementing lightweight proxies for objects or dynamically compiling or loading missing code (Pharo By Example, p. 313).

In Pharo, every operation is a message send, and there are no operators, only messages sent to objects. Therefore, understanding the message syntax is crucial for effectively working with the Pharo system (Pharo By Example, p. 71). By intercepting messages and modifying behavior at runtime, developers can customize the handling of errors like messages not being understood (Pharo By Example, pp. 312-313).",190,,,,,0.625,0.7325840430184656,5,"[""Understanding message syntax Although Pharo 's message syntax is simple , it is unconventional and can take some time getting used to ."", 'The default implementation , i.e. , Object>>doesNotUnderstand : will trigger the debugger at this point .', 'There is no operators , just messages sent to objects .', 'Two very common applications of this technique are 1 ) to implement lightweight proxies for objects , and 2 ) to dynamically compile or load missing code .', 'One could easily override the method Object>>doesNotUnderstand : in any subclass of Object and provide a different way of handling the error .']","['Therefore , understanding the message syntax is crucial for effectively working with the Pharo system ( Pharo By Example , p. 71 ) .', 'If a method is not found in the inheritance chain , the object will send itself the message doesNotUnderstand , triggering the debugger ( Pharo By Example , pp .)', 'In Pharo , every operation is a message send , and there are no operators , only messages sent to objects .', 'This can be useful for implementing lightweight proxies for objects or dynamically compiling or loading missing code ( Pharo By Example , p. 313 ) .', 'This allows developers to intercept errors and provide alternative actions by overriding the Object>>doesNotUnderstand : method in subclasses of Object ( Pharo By Example , p. 107 ) .', 'One way to fix the issue of a message not being understood is to override the doesNotUnderstand : method in a subclass of Object to provide a different way of handling the error ( Pharo By Example , pp .)']",188,1404,263,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

,

In Squeak you can use CommandShell, but I don't know what (if anything) is available for Pharo at this time.

,To invoke shell commands from Squeak or Pharo, you can use the command line interface. In Linux, you can navigate to the unzipped Pharo folder and use the command ""./pharo shared/Pharo5.0.image"" to launch Pharo (Pharo By Example, p. 10). Similarly, on Mac OS X, you can access the Pharo5.0.app bundle and use the command ""Pharo5.0.app/Contents/MacOS/Pharo"" to launch Pharo (Pharo By Example, p. 10).

Pharo tries to ""do the right thing"" when launching, but if you have multiple VMs installed, it may not be able to guess the correct one. In such cases, it is safer to specify the image file you want to launch either by dragging and dropping it onto the VM or specifying it on the command line (Pharo By Example, p. 10).

Additionally, PharoLauncher is a useful tool for downloading and managing Pharo images, making it easy to get new versions and updates (Pharo By Example, p. 10-11). You can also use handy scripts provided on the Pharo website to automate the process of downloading specific versions of Pharo (Pharo By Example, p. 10).

Overall, Pharo does not require installation on your system and can run standalone. Simply download the appropriate zip file for your platform, uncompress it in a directory of your choice, and you are ready to launch Pharo (Pharo By Example, p. 7-8). It is recommended to keep backup copies of the downloaded .image and .changes files for a fresh start, or use a version control system for easier code backup and tracking (Pharo By Example, p. vi).",251,,,,,0.7272727272727273,0.7816453706009775,8,"[""For Linux , assuming that you 're in the unzipped pharo5.0"", 'uncompress it in a directory of your choice now you are ready to launch Pharo .', 'Depending on your platform , download the appropriate zip file , uncompress it in a directory of your choice and now you are ready to launch Pharo .', ""For OS X , assuming that you 're in the directory with the unzipped Pharo5.0.app bundle : When using a Pharo bundle , you need to right - RAG,How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak),"

I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31)
column3: and a four-digit integer for the year.

column1: ( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )
Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?

,

If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression
and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).

,To set day, month, and year values for a Date or DateAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak), you can use regular expressions to parse a date string into its components. For example, you can use a regular expression like '(Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)\s+(\d\d?)\s*,\s*19(\d\d)' to extract the year, month, and day strings from a date string (Pharo By Example, pp. 343-344).

Additionally, you can create a new Date or DateAndTime object by supplying the day, month, and year values individually. While there may not be a single method that sets all three parameters at once, you can use methods like Integer>>asYear to set the year value (Pharo By Example, p. 59). For example, you can set the day, month, and year values separately like this:
date := Date newDay: day month: month year: year.

By using regular expressions and individual methods to set the day, month, and year values, you can effectively create and manipulate Date and DateAndTime objects in Smalltalk.",161,,,,,0.3333333333333333,0.5981892894808886,1,"[""21 343 Regular expressions in Pharo As a more elaborate example , the following piece of code uses a MMM DD , YYYY date format recognizer to convert a date to a three - element array with year , month , and day strings : | date result | date : = ' ( Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)\\s+(\\d\\d?)\\s*,\\s*19(\\d\\d ) ' asRegex .""]","['` ` `    By using regular expressions and individual methods to set the day , month , and year values , you can effectively create and manipulate Date and DateAndTime objects in Smalltalk .', ""For example , you can use a regular expression like ' ( Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)\\s+(\\d\\d?)\\s*,\\s*19(\\d\\d ) ' to extract the year , month , and day strings from a date string ( Pharo By Example , pp . 343 - 344 ) .""]",216,1400,274,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

RAG,How to check if two arrays contain the same elements?,"

Is there any simple way to check if two arrays contain the same elements?
Here is my try were I return true if arrays are not the same:
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.

,

If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.
If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.
Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.
If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.

,To check if two arrays contain the same elements, you can compare the arrays element by element. One way to do this is by iterating over each element in one array and checking if it exists in the other array. If any element is missing, the arrays are not the same (Pharo By Example, p. 182).
For example, you can compare two arrays like this:
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.

It is important to note that when comparing arrays, you should consider redefining the `=` method but also redefining the `hash` method to avoid losing elements or strange behavior (Pharo By Example, p. 218). Additionally, be cautious when using mutable objects as elements in sets or dictionaries, as their hash value can change over time, leading to potential debugging challenges (Pharo By Example, p. 218).",175,,,,,0.1666666666666666,0.564209400356925,1,"['but also hash as follows : Another nasty problem arises if you use a mutable object , i.e. , an object that can change its hash value over time , as an element of a Set or as a key to a Dictionary .']","['Additionally , be cautious when using mutable objects as elements in sets or dictionaries , as their hash value can change over time , leading to potential debugging challenges ( Pharo By Example , p. 218 ) .']",238,1358,258,0.0,[],pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

RAG+CoI,Squeak or Pharo for the beginning Smalltalker?,"

I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.
Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?

,

To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.
Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.

Pharo reinvents the essence of Smalltalk and favors evolutionary and incremental changes over big leaps, ensuring stability while experimenting (Pharo By Example, p. 2). In terms of starting with Seaside, a web development framework, Pharo offers a convenient web-based development interface, making it easy to develop applications iteratively, debug interactively, and recompile and extend applications while the server is running (Pharo By Example, p. 260). ,

From a practical viewpoint the answer would be no, it is not possible to change the size of a String. You can modify the characters of the String though:
Therefore, when you concatenate two strings you get a third one, while the others two remain unchanged

Having said this, there is actually a way to do grow (or shrink) a String. The idea is to use become: (or becomeForward:). This message will replace all references to the receiver with references to the argument. In your case:

The reason why I started my answer by saying that you cannot change the string's size is because in the vast majority of cases the use of become: is overkilling and the recommended practice is to review the code and eliminate the need for modifying the structure of an object.

,In Pharo, it is possible to add a string to a String instance via a method. One way to achieve this is by concatenating two instances of String with a comma, as shown in the example: `s := 'no', ' ', 'worries'.` This will result in the string 'no worries' (p. 210). Additionally, you can modify an existing string using the `at:put:` message, where you can replace characters at specific positions in the string (p. 210).

To define a global in Smalltalk, you can perform an assignment to a capitalized but undeclared identifier, and the parser will offer to declare the global for you. Alternatively, you can programmatically define a global by executing `Smalltalk globals at: #AGlobalName put: nil` and remove it using `Smalltalk globals removeKey: #AGlobalName` (p. 109).

In Pharo, a String is a collection of Characters that is sequenceable, indexable, mutable, and homogeneous, containing only Character instances. Strings are normally created by directly specifying a String literal within single quotes, but the usual collection creation methods will work as well (p. 209).

To modify a string using `replaceAll:with:`, you can replace all occurrences of a specific character or substring with another character or substring. For example, `s replaceAll: $n with: $N` will replace all occurrences of 'n' with 'N' in the string (p. 210).

The syntax for creating a String and a SortedCollection in Pharo differs. Strings are created by directly specifying a String literal within single quotes, while SortedCollections are created by creating a new instance and adding elements to it, or by sending the conversion message `asSortedCollection` to an existing collection (p. 208-209).

,

,In Pharo, you can check if an object is an instance of a given class or a subclass of it using the `isKindOf:` method. This method, defined in the `Object` class, answers whether the receiver's class is either the same as the argument class or a subclass of it (Pharo By Example, p. 183-184). For example, you can use `1 isKindOf: SmallInteger` to check if the object `1` is an instance of the `SmallInteger` class, which would return `true`. Similarly, you can use `1 isKindOf: Number` to check if the object is an instance of the `Number` class, which would also return `true`.

Additionally, you can query the class of an object using the `class` message. Every object in Pharo has a class, and you can find out which class an object belongs to by sending it the `class` message (Pharo By Example, p. 183). For example, sending the message `1 class` would return `SmallInteger`, indicating that the object `1` belongs to the `SmallInteger` class.

Furthermore, it's important to note that in Pharo, classes are also objects. Every class is an instance of a metaclass, and classes define the structure of their instances via private instance variables and the behavior of their instances via public methods (Pharo By Example, p. 88). This object model in Pharo captures the essence of object-oriented programming, where classes are treated as first-class objects that can be manipulated and interacted with like any other object in the system.",241,,,,,0.4444444444444444,0.6925972762655651,4,"['You can ask any object about its class using the message class .', '6.3 Every object is an instance of a class Every object has a class

I've tried the code

,

You can't create a SmallInteger using new:. You already have the number you want to print. It's already a SmallInteger. Just use it like such:

,To print an integer to the Transcript in Pharo, you can use the `Transcript show:` method followed by the integer converted to a string using `printString` (Pharo By Example, p. 16). However, it's important to note that when running code in a Playground, you should use the `Print it` option instead of `Do it` to see the result displayed in the Transcript (Pharo By Example, pp. 17-18).

In Smalltalk (and couple other languages such as Ruby) you have one extra option and that is class extension. You can design your own ""if"" statements that match well your particular domain and makes the code more obvious.
E.g. in your given example, I could add a new method to the Number class called compareTo:lesser:equal:greater:, and then your code changes to

,In Pharo, control constructs are expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections with blocks as arguments, as there is no special syntax for control constructs (Pharo By Example, p. 17). For conditional statements, such as if-else, you can use messages like ifTrue:, ifFalse:, or ifTrue:ifFalse: to the result of a boolean expression (Pharo By Example, p. 17).
To write if-elseif-else statements in Pharo, you can chain multiple ifTrue: and ifFalse: messages together. For example:

In Pharo, the conditional branches of ifTrue: or ifTrue:ifFalse: require blocks, and both the receiver and the argument of the whileTrue: message also require the use of square brackets since the number of executions is unknown (Pharo By Example, p. 83). This approach allows for a more flexible and expressive way of handling control flow in Pharo.",257,,,,,0.5,0.6991760244900775,2,"['For instance , instead of an if - then - else control structure , conditionals are expressed as messages ( such as ifTrue : ) sent to Boolean objects .', 'Following this principle , the conditional branches of ifTrue : or ifTrue : ifFalse : require blocks .']","['In Pharo , control constructs are expressed by sending messages to booleans , numbers , and collections with blocks as arguments , as there is no special syntax for control constructs ( Pharo By Example , p. 17 ) .', '[                   ( x > 25 )                       ifTrue : [ DoSomething ]                       ifFalse : [ DoSomething else ]               ]       ]   ` ` `    In Pharo , the conditional branches of ifTrue : or ifTrue : ifFalse : require blocks , and both the receiver and the argument of the whileTrue : message also require the use of square brackets since the number of executions is unknown ( Pharo By Example , p. 83 ) .', 'For conditional statements , such as if - else , you can use messages like ifTrue : , ifFalse : , or ifTrue : ifFalse : to the result of a boolean expression ( Pharo By Example , p. 17 ) .']",200,6066,267,5.0,"['What is Pharo?', 'Instead, how are control constructs expressed in Pharo?', 'What requires the use of square brackets in Smalltalk for the conditional branches of ifTrue: and ifTrue:ifFalse:?', 'How are the conditional branches of ifTrue: and ifFalse: in Smalltalk implemented?', 'How can control flow be expressed in Seaside?']",pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.
Now in general about what you should know.

• You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.

,In Pharo, the convention for abstract methods is to use the expression `self subclassResponsibility` in the body of the method (Pharo By Example, p. 97). This convention indicates that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method, and these methods should always be overridden to avoid exceptions (Pharo By Example, p. 97). By convention, methods that send `self subclassResponsibility` are considered to be abstract, and if not overridden, an exception will be raised when executed (Pharo By Example, p. 97).

When implementing a method like `size` in a superclass, such as `AbstractArrayDictionary`, using `self subclassResponsibility`, it signals to subclasses that they should provide their own implementation of the method (Pharo By Example, p. 97). This approach ensures that each subclass defines a concrete version of the method, promoting better code organization and adherence to the class hierarchy.

Using `self size` instead of `self subclassResponsibility` in the superclass method would not enforce the requirement for subclasses to provide their own implementation. It would lead to potential inconsistencies and could result in unexpected behavior if a subclass fails to override the method (Pharo By Example, p. 97).

Therefore, following the convention of using `self subclassResponsibility` in abstract methods like `size` ensures that subclasses adhere to the expected behavior and maintain consistency within the class hierarchy. It promotes better code design and helps prevent errors that may arise from missing implementations in subclasses.",231,,,,,0.4,0.6847265810215353,2,"['Instead , by convention , the body of an abstract method consists of the expression self subclassResponsibility .', 'This indicates that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method .']","['Therefore , following the convention of using ` self subclassResponsibility ` in abstract methods like ` size ` ensures that subclasses adhere to the expected behavior and maintain consistency within the class hierarchy .', 'This convention indicates that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method , and these methods should always be overridden to avoid exceptions ( Pharo By Example , p. 97 ) .', 'By convention , methods that send ` self subclassResponsibility ` are considered to be abstract , and if not overridden , an exception will be raised when executed ( Pharo By Example , p. 97 ) .', 'In Pharo , the convention for abstract methods is to use the expression ` self subclassResponsibility ` in the body of the method ( Pharo By Example , p. 97 ) .', 'This approach ensures that each subclass defines a concrete version of the method , promoting better code organization and adherence to the class hierarchy .']",643,5290,307,5.0,"['Why are methods that send self subclassResponsibility abstract by convention?', 'When is the pseudovariable self used in Pharo?', 'What is Object>>subclassResponsibility?', 'By what convention are methods that send self subclassResponsibility considered?', 'What does Self should do in the context of SUnit testing?']",pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

I guess you misunderstand the method syntax a bit, because createNewUser:Arguments part makes no sense. What you should have is a method on the class side like this:

In my example name:email:phone: is the method's selector and userName, userEmail and userPhone are parameters. You can call this method as in your example. name and email are either class side on instance side variables depending on where the method is defined.

Also you shouldn't name a class CreateUser. Think about this, what will be the instances called? ""createUsers""? Usually you name a class User, then you can think about instances as ""users"", and then the responsibility of the class object is ""to create users (its instances)"".
