,To create class (static) methods in Pharo 4.0, you should define them on the class side of the class. For instance, if you have a class called CreateUser with instance variables name, email, and phone, the method declaration should be as follows: `CreateUser class>>createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone` (Pharo By Example, p. 92). To call this static method, you should use the class name followed by the method name and arguments, like this:

If you want to create an instance variable by the same name, the method declaration will be the same but on the instance side of the class. When calling this method to create a new instance, you should use the keyword ""new"" like this:

If you encounter an error message like ""MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >> name:email:phone:"", it means there is a mismatch in the method declaration. Make sure the method is declared correctly on the class side as `CreateUser>>name:email:phone:` (Pharo By Example, p. 92).

In Pharo, instance variables are private to the instance itself, unlike in Java and C++ where they can be accessed by any other instance of the same class. Accessor methods must be defined in Pharo to access instance variables of a class (Pharo By Example, p. 89). Class methods in Pharo are dynamically-dispatched, allowing for inheritance, overriding, and super-sends, unlike static methods in Java (Pharo By Example, p. 92).

To access instance variables in Pharo, you can add accessor methods for class/instance variables and then call them with the class instance or class object instance (Pharo By Example, p. 89).",301,,,,,0.6666666666666666,0.7246638814936216,4,"['In Pharo , all instance variables are private to the object that owns them , so the only way for another object to read or write those variables is through accessor methods like this one .', ""In Pharo , two instances of the same class can not access each other 's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods ."", 'Instance variables Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself .', ""This means that inheritance , overriding and super - sends work for class methods in Pharo , whereas they do n't work for static methods in Java .""]","['To access instance variables in Pharo , you can add accessor methods for class / instance variables and then call them with the class instance or class object instance ( Pharo By Example , p. 89 ) .', 'Accessor methods must be defined in Pharo to access instance variables of a class ( Pharo By Example , p. 89 ) .', 'In Pharo , instance variables are private to the instance itself , unlike in Java and C++ where they can be accessed by any other instance of the same class .', 'Class methods in Pharo are dynamically - dispatched , allowing for inheritance , overriding , and super - sends , unlike static methods in Java ( Pharo By Example , p. 92 ) .']",522,5245,424,5.0,"['What are instance variables in Pharo?', 'What is the distinction between instance variables in Pharo and in Java/C++?', 'What is Pharo?', 'What are class variables in the Pharo object model?', ""What is Pharo's syntax characterized by?""]",pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

RAG+CoI,"If my Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, how do I break out of the loop?","

Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!
I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?

,

Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.
If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the
• „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
• open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.

,If your Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, there are several ways to break out of it. One method is to interrupt the loop and open a debugger on the computation by typing CMD-. This will bring up a pre-debugger window where you can examine the execution and potentially fix the issue (Pharo By Example, p. 140).

Another way to set a breakpoint in Pharo without using self halt is to use the Object>>haltIf: method. This allows you to conditionally halt only if you are in a context of interest. By setting a breakpoint using this method, you can avoid freezing the image and continue running your code without interruption (Pharo By Example, pp. 311-312).

If your Pharo image crashes, you can recover your code by restarting Pharo from the most recent snapshot and selecting ""Recover lost changes"" from the Tools menu. This will allow you to browse through your recent changes and recover any lost work. Additionally, you can use the ChangeList browseRecentLog or browseRecent methods to navigate through your code history and recover specific changes (Pharo By Example, pp. 156-157).

In the Pharo environment, you can also get into the debugger by running a failing test, typing CMD-. to interrupt a running process, or inserting a Halt now expression in your code. The debugger in Pharo allows you to step through code, inspect objects, evaluate code, and even change code while debugging, providing a powerful tool for troubleshooting and fixing issues in your code (Pharo By Example, p. 116).

Overall, Pharo offers a robust set of tools and features to help you debug and troubleshoot issues in your code, ensuring that you can efficiently address any problems that may arise during development.",281,,,,,0.6923076923076923,0.7342895469630025,9,"['Intelligent breakpoints The Pharo way to set a breakpoint is to evaluate self halt at an interesting point in a method .', 'If you execute code that goes into an infinite loop , you can interrupt it and open a debugger on the computation by typing CMD- .', 'What 311 Reflection we need is a way to conditionally halt only if we are in a context of interest .', 'When this message is sent , execution will be interrupted and a debugger will open to this point in your program ( see Chapter : The Pharo Environment for more details about the debugger ) .', 'How to get your code back Restart Pharo from the most recent snapshot , and select World > Tools ...', 'The debugger is not launched from a menu

column1: ,To create a class in Pharo Smalltalk, you need to write Smalltalk code in the System Browser. You can create a new class by editing the class creation template, replacing Object with the existing class name you want to subclass and NameOfSubclass with the desired name for your new subclass. You can also define instance variables and methods within the class (Pharo By Example, pp. 119-120).

In Pharo, the instance side is where you create methods that work when you instantiate an object, while the class side does not require an object to function, similar to static methods in Java (Pharo By Example, various pages).

When creating a new class, it is important to understand that each class in Pharo inherits its behavior and structure from a single superclass. The root of the inheritance hierarchy is ProtoObject, but classes should normally inherit from Object or its subclasses. Classes in Pharo inherit from a single superclass, supporting single inheritance (Pharo By Example, pp. 96-97).

Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself, and two instances of the same class cannot access each other's instance variables unless accessor methods are defined. Access to instance variables is through instance methods of the class that defines them, similar to protected variables in C++ and Java (Pharo By Example, pp. 89).

To define class instance variables, each class has its own private set of variables, and subclasses inherit these variables but have their own private copies. Class instance variables are accessed through class methods, providing each class with its own set of private variables (Pharo By Example, pp. 92).

In summary, to create a class in Pharo Smalltalk, you need to write Smalltalk code in the System Browser, define instance variables and methods, understand inheritance from a single superclass, and manage instance and class variables appropriately.",298,,,,,1.0,0.7946305440279225,10,"['You create a new class by editing this template : replace Object by the name of the existing class of which you ,To generate getter/setter code automatically for a class in Pharo or Squeak, you can follow the convention where a getter method has the same name as the variable it gets, and a setter is named similarly but with a trailing "":"" (Pharo By Example, p. 49). You can dynamically add accessors for instance variables on demand by creating a class that automatically adds accessors for its instance variables (Pharo By Example, p. 315).

To define instance variables and methods in Pharo, you need to write Smalltalk code. You can create a new class by editing a class creation template, replacing the superclass name and subclass name, and filling in the instance variable names (Pharo By Example, pp. 119-120). Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself, and accessors must be defined if access is needed (Pharo By Example, p. 89).

You can access instance variables by name in any instance method of the class that defines them or in methods defined in its subclasses (Pharo By Example, p. 89). Class instance variables are private to the class itself, and subclasses inherit their own private copies of these variables (Pharo By Example, p. 92).

RAG+CoI,"Why is Smalltalk sometimes called ""dynamically typed""?","

,

""Dynamically typed"" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean ""dynamically checked"". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.
See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.

,Smalltalk is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"" because it is a dynamically-typed language, meaning that there are no type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns (Pharo By Example, p. 1). In Smalltalk, nearly everything happens by sending messages to objects, and there are only dynamic message sends, with no static class operations (Pharo By Example, p. 100). When sending a message in Smalltalk, the object, and not the sender, selects the appropriate method for responding to the message, and the method associated with the message is looked up dynamically (Pharo By Example, p. 14). This dynamic message sending approach in Smalltalk encourages a style where objects tend to have very small methods and delegate tasks to other objects, rather than implementing large procedural methods (Pharo By Example, p. 100).

In summary, Smalltalk is called ""dynamically typed"" because it is a dynamically-typed language where type information is determined at runtime through message passing to objects, rather than through static type annotations (Pharo By Example, p. 1, 100).",165,,,,,0.6,0.7083830305534252,3,"['Ok , so nearly everything in Pharo happens by sending messages .', ""One of the consequences of Pharo 's model of message sending is that it encourages a style in which objects tend to have very small methods and delegate tasks to other objects , rather than implementing huge , procedural methods that assume too much responsibility ."", 'When sending a message to an object , the object , and not the sender , selects the appropriate method for responding to your message .']","['In Smalltalk , nearly everything happens by sending messages to objects , and there are only dynamic message sends , with no static class operations ( Pharo By Example , p. 100 ) .', 'When sending a message in Smalltalk , the object , and not the sender , selects the appropriate method for responding to the message , and the method associated with the message is looked up dynamically ( Pharo By Example , p. 14 ) .', 'This dynamic message sending approach in Smalltalk encourages a style where objects tend to have very small methods and delegate tasks to other objects , rather than implementing large procedural methods ( Pharo By Example , p. 100 ) .']",224,6031,228,5.0,"['Why is Pharo a reflective system?', 'What is one of the unique features of Pharo (and its ancestors)?', 'Why is Pharo based on sending messages to objects?', 'Which programming language is Pharo based on?', 'Why is the syntax of Pharo so simple?']",pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

,In Pharo, the difference between ""instance Variable Names"" and ""class Variable Names"" lies in their accessibility and scope. Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself, meaning that they cannot be accessed by other instances of the same class unless accessor methods are defined (Pharo By Example, p. 89). This encapsulation boundary at the instance level ensures data integrity and security within objects.

On the other hand, class variables in Pharo are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself. They have the same lifetime as the class and are accessible by all instances of the class. Class variables allow for data sharing and are indicated by names starting with a capital letter (Pharo By Example, pp. 109-110). They provide a way to maintain shared data across instances and classes.

Furthermore, class instance variables and class methods in Pharo operate on classes as objects. Class instance variables describe the state of classes (instances of metaclasses), while class methods define behavior for classes. These elements are similar to ordinary instance variables and methods but operate on classes instead of instances (Pharo By Example, p. 90). The distinction between instance variables and class variables in Pharo ensures proper data encapsulation and organization within the object-oriented framework.",207,,,,,0.6666666666666666,0.7271141288999912,6,"['Instance variables Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself .', 'Class variables are shared between a class , its subclasses and its instances .', 'Similarly , class instance variables are just instance variables defined by a metaclass ( and describe the state of classes - RAG+CoI,"Why do methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk?","

Background
In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or ""self"" in the message context).

column3: is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

column1: • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?

,

One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows ""self"". By returning ""self"" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the ""self"" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning ""self"" is the most elegant thing to do.

,In Smalltalk, methods return ""self"" by default because the pseudo-variable ""self"" represents the receiver of the message, similar to the keyword ""this"" in Java (Pharo By Example, p. 103). When a method does not explicitly return a value, it implicitly returns the object that received the message, which is represented by ""self"" (Pharo By Example, p. 103). This design choice encourages a style where objects have small methods and delegate tasks to other objects, promoting better object-oriented design practices (Pharo By Example, p. 100).

The idea behind this default behavior is to simplify method implementation and promote message passing as the primary means of communication between objects in Pharo (Pharo By Example, p. 85). By default, a method returns the message receiver, allowing for a more streamlined and consistent approach to method invocation and object interaction (Pharo By Example, p. 103).

The philosophical background behind returning ""self"" by default is to emphasize the object-oriented nature of Smalltalk and Pharo, where everything is an object and communication happens through message passing (Pharo By Example, p. 85). This approach aligns with the principles of object-oriented programming, encouraging encapsulation, modularity, and delegation of responsibilities (Pharo By Example, p. 113).

Practically, returning ""self"" by default simplifies method chaining and promotes a more cohesive and modular design in Pharo applications (Pharo By Example, p. 103). By following this convention, developers can focus on creating small, focused methods that collaborate effectively through message passing, leading to more maintainable and extensible codebases (Pharo By Example, p. 106).",247,,,,,1.0,0.7817690985086858,4,"['Important By default ( if not specified differently ) a method returns the message receiver .', 'We usually say that the method answers self , because in Pharo the pseudovariable self represents the receiver of the message , much like the keyword this in Java .', 'Again , this is not the case in Pharo , instead you can imagine that a method without an explicit return ends with ^ self .', ""One of the consequences of Pharo 's model of message sending is that it encourages a style in which objects tend to have very small methods and delegate tasks to other objects , rather than implementing huge , procedural methods that assume too much responsibility .""]","['By default , a method returns the message receiver , allowing for a more streamlined and consistent approach to method invocation and object interaction ( Pharo By Example , p. 103 ) .', 'In Smalltalk , methods return "" self "" by default because the pseudo - variable "" self "" represents the receiver of the message , similar to the keyword "" this "" in Java ( Pharo By Example , p. 103 ) .', 'When a method does not explicitly return a value , it implicitly returns the object that received the message , which is represented by "" self "" ( Pharo By Example , p. 103 ) .', 'This design choice encourages a style where objects have small methods and delegate tasks to other objects , promoting better object - You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.

,When an object in Pharo receives a message, it first looks for a method with the same name as the message in its class. If the method is not found, it continues to search up the inheritance chain until it reaches the Object class. If the method is still not found, the object sends itself the message `doesNotUnderstand:` with the message selector as its argument (Pharo By Example, pp. 312-313). This process allows developers to intercept errors and handle them differently by overriding the `doesNotUnderstand:` method in subclasses of Object (Pharo By Example, pp. 107-108).

The `doesNotUnderstand:` method, when invoked, creates a new `MessageNotUnderstood` object that can start a debugger in the current execution context (Pharo By Example, pp. 107). This method can be overridden to provide alternative behavior, such as implementing lightweight proxies for objects or dynamically compiling or loading missing code (Pharo By Example, pp. 313).

In Pharo, everything happens by sending messages to objects. The Pharo objects communicate by sending messages to each other, and methods are dynamically looked up (late-bound) (Pharo By Example, pp. 85-87). If a method is not found for a message, the object will send itself the `doesNotUnderstand:` message, allowing for alternative strategies to be implemented for handling the message (Pharo By Example, pp. 101).

Overall, the `doesNotUnderstand:` method plays a crucial role in handling messages that do not have corresponding methods, providing developers with the flexibility to customize error handling and Pharo does not have a built-in system() function like in Unix languages to run external shell commands. Is there any simple way to check if two arrays contain the same elements?
Here is my try were I return true if arrays are not the same:

,

If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.
If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.
Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.
If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.

,To check if two arrays contain the same elements, a comparison can be made by iterating over the elements of both arrays and checking for equality. One way to do this is by comparing the sizes of the arrays first and then iterating over each element to check for equality (Pharo By Example, p. 182).
For example, consider the following code snippet:
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.

In this code snippet, `arr1` and `arr2` are two arrays being compared. The code checks if the sizes of the arrays are equal and then iterates over each element of `arr1` to check if it exists in `arr2`. If any element is not found in `arr2`, the function returns `true`, indicating that the arrays do not contain the same elements.

It is important to note that when overriding the equality operator `=`, the hash method should also be redefined to ensure consistency (Pharo By Example, p. 183). Additionally, it is recommended not to override the identity operator `==`, as its semantics are consistent across all classes (Pharo By Example, p. 183).

In conclusion, to check if two arrays contain the same elements, a comparison based on size and element equality can be implemented, ensuring that both the equality and hash methods are appropriately defined for consistency.",251,,,,,0.0,0.5535627140832904,0,[],[],238,4397,352,2.0,"['How is object equality determined in Smalltalk?', 'In what way do these collections handle their elements?']",pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

RAG (OpenAI),Squeak or Pharo for the beginning Smalltalker?,"

I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.
Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?

,

To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.
Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.

,When considering whether to start with Squeak or Pharo as a beginner Smalltalker, it's important to note that Pharo is a modern, open-source language inspired by Smalltalk, offering a dynamically-typed language with a simple syntax that is readable even for those unfamiliar with it. Pharo provides a live coding environment that allows code modification while it executes, without disrupting the workflow, and a powerful IDE to manage complex code effectively【4:6†source】.

Pharo's approach involves evolutionary and incremental changes rather than a Big Bang style, which helps maintain stability while experimenting with new features and libraries. This approach fosters community contributions and rapid feedback, contributing to Pharo's success. Additionally, Pharo integrates changes made by the community daily, ensuring continuous improvement and innovation【4:2†source】.

On the other hand, Squeak is another popular implementation of Smalltalk. While both Squeak and Pharo have their strengths, Pharo's emphasis on modernity, live coding support, and a powerful IDE may make it a more attractive choice for beginners looking to experiment with Smalltalk. However, it's essential to consider personal preferences and specific project requirements when choosing between the two implementations【4:6†source】.

In conclusion, for a beginner Smalltalker coming from a Ruby background, Pharo's modern features, live coding support, and powerful IDE make it a compelling choice to start experimenting with Smalltalk programming【4:6†source】.",209,,,,,0.4285714285714285,0.6738371688465732,3,"['Finally Pharo is not read - only , Pharo integrates changes made by the community , daily .', 'A live coding environment that allows the programmer to modify code while the code executes , without any need to slow down their workflow .', 'This facilitates contributions and rapid feedback from the community , on which Pharo relies on for its success .']","['Additionally , Pharo integrates changes made by the community daily , ensuring continuous improvement and innovation[(4:2+source )]  .', 'Pharo provides a live coding environment that allows code modification while it executes , without disrupting the workflow , and a powerful IDE to manage complex code effectively[(4:6+source )]  .', ""This approach fosters community contributions and rapid feedback , contributing to Pharo 's success .""]",183,3432,301,,,pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

,

From a practical viewpoint the answer would be no, it is not possible to change the size of a String. You can modify the characters of the String though:
Therefore, when you concatenate two strings you get a third one, while the others two remain unchanged

Having said this, there is actually a way to do grow (or shrink) a String. The idea is to use become: (or becomeForward:). This message will replace all references to the receiver with references to the argument. In your case:

,In Smalltalk, it is possible to modify a String instance using methods like `at:put:` to change specific characters within the string. For example, you can change characters at specific positions within a string using `at:put:` as demonstrated in the Pharo By Example textbook (Pharo By Example 5.pdf, p. 1) where the string 'no hurries' is modified by changing characters at positions 4 and 5.

Additionally, strings in Smalltalk can be concatenated using the comma operator. Two instances of String can be concatenated by using a comma between them, as shown in the textbook (Pharo By Example 5.pdf, p. 2) where the string 'no' and 'worries' are concatenated to form 'no worries'.

Furthermore, Smalltalk provides methods like `replaceAll:with:` and `replaceFrom:to:with:` to modify existing strings. These methods allow for replacing specific characters or intervals within a string, as illustrated in the textbook (Pharo By Example 5.pdf, p. 4) where the string 'No hurries' is modified by replacing characters and intervals.

Therefore, in Smalltalk, while there isn't a direct method like `append` to add a string to a String instance, you can achieve similar functionality by using methods like `at:put:`, string concatenation with the comma operator, `replaceAll:with:`, and `replaceFrom:to:with:` to modify and combine strings as needed【4:0†source】【4:2†source】.",200,,,,,0.2,0.6410752310160879,1,['We can also modify an existing string using replaceAll : with : or replaceFrom : to : with : as shown below .'],"['Furthermore , Smalltalk provides methods like ` replaceAll : with : ` and ` replaceFrom : to : with : ` to modify existing strings .']",192,3409,316,,,pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

RAG (OpenAI),What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?,"

In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

,

The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.
meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.
The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.
Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:

which is a little bit more verbose.

,In Smalltalk, the terms ""self"" and ""yourself"" have distinct meanings within a method body. The keyword ""self"" in Smalltalk represents the receiver of the message, similar to the keyword ""this"" in Java. When a method in Pharo reaches its end without explicitly returning a value using the ^ construct, it implicitly answers the object that received the message, which is referred to as ""self"" in Pharo【4:0†source】. On the other hand, ""yourself"" is used in Smalltalk to return the modified object itself. For example, in the context of mutating a string, using ""yourself"" ensures that the modified string is returned, as opposed to mutating the original string directly【4:1†source】.

To further clarify the difference between ""self"" and ""yourself"" in Smalltalk, consider the scenario where a method is defined in a class and later inherited by a subclass. Using ""self"" in this context ensures that the method will be bound to the respective receiver, maintaining the correct behavior even in subclasses. This distinction becomes crucial when subclasses inherit methods, as each class will have a different value for their instance variables, and using ""self"" ensures the correct binding to the respective receiver class【4:4†source】.

In summary, ""self"" in Smalltalk refers to the receiver of the message within a method, while ""yourself"" is used to return the modified object itself, ensuring that the original object remains unchanged. Understanding and correctly applying these keywords are essential in Smalltalk programming to manage object behavior and method responses effectively.",239,,,,,0.4285714285714285,0.6297472558003175,3,"['We usually say that the method answers self , because in Pharo the pseudovariable self represents the receiver of the message , much like the keyword this in Java .', 'Again , this is not the case in Pharo , instead you can imagine that a method without an explicit return ends with ^ self .', '* self always refers to the receiver of the currently executing method .']","['The keyword "" self "" in Smalltalk represents the receiver of the message , similar to the keyword "" this "" in Java .', 'When a method in Pharo reaches its end without explicitly returning a value using the ^ construct , it implicitly answers the object that received the message , which is referred to as "" self "" in Pharo[(4:0+source )]  .', 'Using "" self "" in this context ensures that the method will be bound to the respective receiver , maintaining the correct behavior even in subclasses .']",120,3355,326,,,pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

• We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
• We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).

There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

,In Pharo Smalltalk, to initialize variables, it is recommended to follow a specific practice. When implementing an initialization method, it is good practice to start by sending `super initialize` before performing any further initialization. This ensures that inherited instance variables are properly initialized, as the knowledge of how to initialize them is already captured in the `initialize` methods of each superclass in the inheritance chain. By sending `super initialize`, you compose inherited behavior that would otherwise be overridden, promoting a structured and efficient initialization process【4:3†source】.

Additionally, in Pharo Smalltalk, instance initialization methods must always be defined on the instance side. The class side does not have access to instance variables and therefore cannot initialize them directly. Instead, the class can send initialization messages, using accessors, to newly created instances. This approach ensures that instance variables are initialized correctly during object creation【4:6†source】.

,In Smalltalk, accessing class variables involves understanding the distinction between instance variables and class variables. Class variables, also known as shared variables, are accessible by all instances of a class as well as the class itself. They are declared in the class definition template and are shared among all instances and the class. For example, in the context of the Color class, variables like ColorRegistry are class variables accessible from both instance and class methods【4:7†source】.

In Smalltalk, instances of a class cannot directly access each other's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods. There is no language syntax that provides direct access to another object's instance variables. Reflection, a mechanism in Smalltalk, allows for accessing another object's instance variables, primarily used for meta-programming tools like object inspectors【4:6†source】.

To access the year variable in the Class1 object start-time in Smalltalk, you would need to consider the use of class variables or accessor methods. Since direct access to another object's instance variables is not provided by the language syntax, you may need to define accessor methods in Class1 to access the year variable in start-time from Class2【4:6†source】.",184,,,,,0.8571428571428571,0.7917483891977583,6,"[""In Pharo , two instances of the same class can not access each other 's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods ."", 'Class variables are shared between a class , its subclasses and its instances .', 'A class variable is declared in the class definition template .', 'For example , the class Color defines a large number of class variables to speed up color creation

I am creating a Pharo Class method that takes 3 arguments. I am using the following code and it gives me the error ""Variable or expression expected..""

,

The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).
So for a method that, in Java or a similar ""curly bracket language"", might look something like this:
you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:
making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a ""symbol"" in a global dictionary):
That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):
Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.
Getting back to your case, you had this:

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says ""Variable or expression expected"" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.
• #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected
• if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side

• think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well
column2: ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)

column1: ,To create a Pharo Class method that takes multiple arguments, you need to follow the syntax for keyword messages in Pharo. The selector for a method with multiple arguments should consist of keywords, each ending in a colon "":"". Here is the correct method declaration syntax for a method with three arguments:
- `MethodName` is the name of the method.
- `arg1` and `arg2` are the keywords representing the arguments.
- `argValue1` and `argValue2` are the values passed for the respective arguments.
Later, when calling this method, you should use the following format:
ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
This format ensures that the method is correctly invoked with the specified arguments. Remember to replace `ClassName` with the actual class name where the method is defined.

This syntax aligns with the conventions for keyword messages in Pharo, where each keyword corresponds to an argument in the method call. For further details on keyword messages and method syntax in Pharo, you can refer to ""Pharo By Example""【4:5†source】【4:5†source】.",175,,,,,0.125,0.5964643507539025,1,['Their selector consists of one or more keywords each ending in a colon ( :)'],"['The selector for a method with multiple arguments should consist of keywords , each ending in a colon "" : "" .']",205,3577,280,,,pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

,To create a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk, you need to follow specific conventions. In Pharo, the traditional way of creating instances involves using the `new:` message to specify the size of a collection or the `with:` message to add elements to a collection. For example, `Array new: 5` creates an array of size 5, while `Array with: 4 with: 3/2 with: 'lulu'` creates an array with specific elements【4:0†source】.

When defining a constructor method in Pharo, it is essential to consider the inheritance chain and the use of `super initialize`. It is recommended to send `super initialize` at the beginning of the initialization method to compose inherited behavior properly. This practice ensures that the superclass's initialization is executed before any further initialization steps are performed【4:2†source】.

In Pharo, the object model is inspired by Smalltalk, where everything is an object, and objects communicate by sending messages to each other. Methods are public and dynamically looked up, emphasizing late binding. Instance variables are private to the object, maintaining encapsulation principles【4:5†source】.

To implement a constructor-like behavior in Pharo, you can define an initialization method within your class. For instance, you can define an `initialize` method in a class like `MyStackMachine` to set up initial values for instance variables. This method typically includes `super initialize` to ensure proper initialization of the superclass and then proceeds with specific initialization steps, such as initializing instance variables like `contents := OrderedCollection new`【4:5†source】【4:6†source】.

Therefore, to simulate a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk, you should define an initialization method within your class, follow the inheritance chain by using `super initialize`, and ensure that your object creation and initialization processes align with the principles of object-oriented programming in Pharo.",275,,,,,0.4166666666666667,0.7314870256707312,5,"['For example , Array new : 4 creates an array of 4 elements .', 'The Pharo object model The Pharo programming model is heavily inspired by the one of Smalltalk .', 'It is therefore good practice whenever implementing an initialize method to send super initialize before performing any further initialization : We need super sends to compose inherited behaviour that would otherwise be overridden .', 'Methods are all public and dynamically looked up ( late - bound ) .', 'Instance variables are private to the object .']","[""For example , ` Array new : 5 ` creates an array of size 5 , while ` Array with : 4 with : 3/2 with : ' lulu ' ` creates an array with specific elements[(4:0+source )]  ."", 'In Pharo , the object model is inspired by Smalltalk , where everything is an object , and objects communicate by sending messages to each other .', 'It is recommended to send ` super initialize ` at the beginning of the initialization method to compose inherited behavior properly .', 'Methods are public and dynamically looked up , emphasizing late binding .', 'Instance variables are private to the object , maintaining encapsulation principles[(4:5+source )]  .']",252,3553,393,,,pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo
RAG (OpenAI),SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript,"<pre><code>|oc|
Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:
When replacing 'self' with 'oc' I am still getting the same error. I am looking for a way to output the collection using the Transcript.
Why can't Transcript be the receiver of my code?

,

Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

,When attempting to use `Transcript` as the receiver in Smalltalk code to output the contents of an `OrderedCollection`, you encountered the error ""MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>>Transcript""【4:0†source】. This error occurs because the `Transcript` class does not understand the message `show:` when sent directly to an `OrderedCollection` object. To output the contents of the collection using `Transcript`, you need to iterate over the elements of the collection and send the `show:` message to each element individually. Here is an example of how you can modify your code to achieve this:
oc do: [:each | Transcript show: each printString

By iterating over the elements of the `OrderedCollection` and sending the `show:` message to each element, you can successfully output the contents of the collection using `Transcript`【4:0†source】【4:1†source】.",131,,,,,0.0,0.5976137411524859,0,[],[],204,3496,222,,,pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

,In Pharo, the convention for defining abstract methods is to use the expression `self subclassResponsibility`, indicating that subclasses are responsible for implementing concrete versions of the method. Abstract methods should always be overridden and never executed. If an abstract method is executed, an exception will be raised, serving as a reminder to implement it in subclasses .

When considering the implementation of the `size` method in classes like `AbstractArrayDictionary`, the use of `self subclassResponsibility` in the method indicates that subclasses must provide a concrete implementation of the method. This approach ensures that each subclass defines its own version of the method, tailored to its specific requirements. By following this convention, the codebase remains flexible and extensible, allowing for variations in behavior among subclasses .

Regarding the specific question about implementing the `size` method in `AbstractArrayDictionary` and potentially in subclasses like `TreeBasedDictionary`, the use of `self subclassResponsibility` in `AbstractArrayDictionary` signals that subclasses must implement their own versions of the `size` method. This approach enforces the principle of encapsulation and ensures that each subclass provides a customized implementation of the method.

In contrast, using `^self size` in the `size` method would lead to a different behavior. This implementation would cause the method to call itself recursively, potentially resulting in an infinite loop if not handled correctly. Therefore, the use of `self subclassResponsibility` is preferred in this context to enforce the requirement for subclasses to define their own `size` method implementations .

In summary, the use of `self subclassResponsibility` in abstract methods like `size` in `AbstractArrayDictionary` serves as a clear indication that subclasses are expected to provide concrete implementations of these methods, ensuring flexibility, extensibility, and adherence to the principles of object-oriented design in Pharo programming .",281,,,,,0.375,0.6520022099610363,3,"['Instead , by convention , the body of an abstract method consists of the expression self subclassResponsibility .', 'self subclassResponsibility methods should always be overridden , and thus should never be executed .', 'This indicates that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method .']","['In Pharo , the convention for defining abstract methods is to use the expression ` self subclassResponsibility ` , indicating that subclasses are responsible for implementing concrete versions of the method .', 'Abstract methods should always be overridden and never executed .', 'This approach ensures that each subclass defines its own version of the method , tailored to its specific requirements .']",631,3925,370,,,pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

column1: ,To create a class in Pharo Smalltalk, you typically follow the process of defining the class and its methods on both the instance side and the class side. In Pharo, everything is an object, and objects communicate by sending messages to each other. Here is a concise guide based on the information from ""Pharo By Example"":
- To create a new class, you can use the `subclass: instanceVariableNames:` message to an existing class. This message creates a subclass with the specified instance variables.
- When defining a class, you can specify instance variables that belong to each instance of the class. These variables are private to the object.

- Instance variables are initialized in the `initialize` method of the class. For example, you can initialize an instance variable `contents` in a class `MyStackMachine` as shown in the textbook【4:3†source】.
- In Pharo, methods are divided into two sides: the instance side and the class side.
- The instance side is where you define methods that will work when you instantiate an object of the class.
- The class side contains methods that do not require an object to be created and are similar to static methods in other languages like Java.
- When adding methods to a class, you can decide whether a method should be placed on the instance side or the class side based on its intended functionality【4:6†source】.
- Methods can be added to a class by selecting the appropriate protocol and replacing the template text with the desired method code.
- Methods on the instance side typically operate on the instance variables of the object and define behavior specific to each instance.
- Methods on the class side are shared among all instances of the class and can be used for class-level operations【4:6†source】.
- In the provided example of creating a Dog class, methods like `bark` and `count` are placed on the class side, which can be appropriate for operations that apply to the class as a whole.

- An example of a method that could be placed on the instance side in this scenario could be a method like `feed` that operates on specific instances of the Dog class, modifying their state or behavior【4:6†source】.

By following these guidelines, you can effectively create classes in Pharo Smalltalk, define their behavior on both the instance and class sides, and organize methods based on their intended functionality.",431,,,,,0.4,0.7038052598742146,4,"['A new class is normally created by sending the message subclass : instanceVariableNames : ...', 'In Pharo you can develop in a traditional way , by defining a class , then its instance variables , then its methods .', 'Instance variables are private to the object .', 'Ok , so nearly everything in Pharo happens by sending messages .']","['This message creates a subclass with the specified instance variables .      - When defining a class , you can specify instance variables that belong to each instance of the class .', 'To create a class in Pharo Smalltalk , you typically follow the process of defining the class and its methods on both the instance side and the class side .', '* * Defining a Class * * :      - To create a new class , you can use the ` subclass : instanceVariableNames : ` message to an existing class .', 'These variables are private to the object .      - Instance variables are initialized in the ` initialize ` method of the class .', 'In Pharo , everything is an object , and objects communicate by sending messages to each other .']",854,4102,533,,,pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

,In Pharo or Squeak, you can automatically generate getter and setter methods for instance variables in a class. One approach involves dynamically adding accessors for instance variables on demand. By implementing a method like the one below, you can achieve this functionality:
self class compile: messageName, String cr, ' ^ ', messageName.

This method checks if the message selector corresponds to an instance variable name. If it does, it compiles a method to return the value of that instance variable, effectively creating a getter method dynamically. This technique can be extended to generate setter methods as well【4:1†source】.

In Pharo, the convention for naming getter and setter methods differs from other languages. A getter method in Pharo has the same name as the variable it retrieves, while a setter method appends a colon to the variable name. For example, a getter for a variable `mouseAction` would be named `mouseAction`, and the setter would be named `mouseAction:`. These methods, collectively known as accessor methods, should be placed in the accessing protocol of the class【4:3†source】.

Furthermore, in Pharo, you have the flexibility to develop interactively by defining instance variables and methods on the fly. This dynamic development approach allows for increased productivity by enabling you to code in the debugger using the context of currently executed objects. RAG (OpenAI),"Why is Smalltalk sometimes called ""dynamically typed""?","

I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called ""dynamically typed"" and not ""untyped"" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.
There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?

,

""Dynamically typed"" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean ""dynamically checked"". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.
See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.

,Smalltalk is sometimes referred to as ""dynamically typed"" because it is a dynamically-typed language where type information is associated with values at runtime rather than at compile time. In Smalltalk, variables, parameters, and method returns do not have explicit type annotations like in statically-typed languages. Instead, the type of an object is determined by its class at runtime. This dynamic typing characteristic allows for flexibility in programming as types are checked during execution, enabling changes to the type of variables without explicit declarations【4:4†source】.

Unlike statically-typed languages where types are checked at compile time, Smalltalk's dynamic typing means that type information is assigned to variables, parameters, and method returns at runtime. This dynamic type checking allows for more flexibility and adaptability during program execution, as types are verified and enforced as the program runs. Therefore, Smalltalk's dynamic typing feature enables developers to work with variables and objects without the need for explicit type declarations, enhancing the language's simplicity and readability【4:4†source】.

In summary, Smalltalk is called ""dynamically typed"" because it assigns type information to variables, parameters, and method returns at runtime based on the class of the objects, allowing for dynamic type checking and flexibility in programming without the need for explicit type annotations【4:4†source】.",200,,,,,0.0,0.5561929347067233,0,[],[],212,3522,269,,,pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

,In Pharo, the terms ""instanceVariableNames"" and ""classVariableNames"" refer to different types of variables used in object-oriented programming.

- **Instance Variable Names**: These are variables specific to instances of a class, meaning each object created from the class has its own set of instance variables. These variables define the state of individual objects. For example, in the Color class, ""rgb"" and ""cachedDepth"" are instance variables, accessible only to instances of Color【4:0†source】.

- **Class Variable Names**: On the other hand, class variables are shared among instances of a class and the class itself, including its subclasses. These variables are defined at the class level and are accessible to all instances of the class and its subclasses. An example from the Color class includes ""ColorRegistry"" and ""CachedColormaps"" as class variables, indicated by their capitalization【4:0†source】.

- **Difference**: The key distinction lies in the scope of these variables. Instance variables are unique to each object instance, holding specific state information for that object. In contrast, class variables are shared across instances of a class and its subclasses, providing a shared state accessible by all instances and the class itself【4:0†source】.

Therefore, in your Pharo class definition, when you specify `instanceVariableNames: ''`, you are indicating that the class does not have any instance-specific variables, and when you specify `classVariableNames: ''`, you are stating that there are no shared class variables defined for the class【4:0†source】.",224,,,,,0.375,0.6837307680241393,3,"['Instance variables are private to the object .', 'Class variables are shared between a class , its subclasses and its instances .', 'In Figure 6 - 27 we see that rgb and cachedDepth are instance variables of Color , hence only accessible to instances of Color .']","['These variables are defined at the class level and are accessible to all instances of the class and its subclasses .', 'In contrast , class variables are shared across instances of a class and its subclasses , providing a shared state accessible by all instances and the class itself[(4:0+source )]  .', 'On the other hand , class variables are shared among instances of a class and the class itself , including its subclasses .', 'For example , in the Color class , "" rgb "" and "" cachedDepth "" are instance variables , accessible only to instances of Color[(4:0+source )]  .    - * * Class Variable Names * * :', 'Instance variables are unique to each object instance , holding specific state information for that object .']",179,3535,323,,,pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

RAG (OpenAI),"Why do methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk?","

Background
In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or ""self"" in the message context).

column3: is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

column1: • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?

,

One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows ""self"". By returning ""self"" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the ""self"" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning ""self"" is the most elegant thing to do.

,In Smalltalk, methods return ""self"" by default if no explicit return statement is provided. This means that if a method reaches its end without a specific return value indicated, it implicitly returns the object that received the message. This behavior is similar to the keyword ""this"" in Java. Unlike languages like Ruby, where the default return is the value of the last statement in the method, Smalltalk's default return is the receiver of the message, represented by ""self"" in Pharo .

The design choice of having methods return ""self"" by default in Smalltalk serves a specific purpose. It simplifies the code by ensuring that a method always answers with a value, which is an object. Even if the method does not have an explicit return statement, it still provides a response – the object that received the message. This default behavior aligns with the principle that a method should always return a value, even if it is the receiver itself .

The idea behind this default return of ""self"" in Smalltalk methods is to maintain consistency and clarity in the language. By having methods implicitly return the receiver object, it streamlines the understanding of message passing and method invocation. This approach also emphasizes the object-oriented nature of Smalltalk, where methods are expected to interact with objects and return meaningful values .

Philosophically, the default return of ""self"" in Smalltalk methods reflects the concept of message passing and object-oriented programming. It reinforces the idea that methods are meant to operate on objects and communicate through messages. By default, returning ""self"" underscores the object-centric nature of Smalltalk and promotes a clear and direct relationship between methods and objects .

Practically, the default return of ""self"" in Smalltalk methods offers several benefits. It simplifies method chaining, where multiple methods can be invoked sequentially on the same object. This default behavior facilitates a fluent and concise coding style, making it easier to write and understand code that involves method cascades and object interactions .

In conclusion, the default return of ""self"" in Smalltalk methods is a deliberate design choice that enhances the clarity, consistency, and object-oriented nature of the language. By implicitly returning the receiver object, Smalltalk promotes a straightforward and intuitive approach to method invocation and object manipulation, ultimately contributing to the elegance and expressiveness of Smalltalk programming .",381,,,,,0.2307692307692307,0.62457046243271,3,"['Again , this is not the case in Pharo , instead you can imagine that a method without an explicit return ends with ^ self .', 'Other languages , such as Ruby , by default return the value of the last statement in the method .', ', the method still answers a value - it answers the object that received the message .']","['In Smalltalk , methods return "" self "" by default if no explicit return statement is provided .', 'Unlike languages like Ruby , where the default return is the value of the last statement in the method , Smalltalk \'s default return is the receiver of the message , represented by "" self "" in Pharo .', 'Even if the method does not have an explicit return statement , it still provides a response - the object that received the message .']",288,3486,464,,,pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

RAG (OpenAI),How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set,"

Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.
data:= received data after each refresh.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
""How to check if this data already exist and not add""
How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?

,

Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.
If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:
<pre><code>(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

RAG (OpenAI),Message Not Understood for existing method,"

Ok, so it's like this:
Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?

,

You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.

,When a message is sent to an object in Pharo, the system first searches for a corresponding method in the method dictionary of the object's class. If no method is found, the system continues to search up the class hierarchy until it reaches the Object class. If the method is still not found, the object sends itself the `doesNotUnderstand:` message, which triggers the `MessageNotUnderstood` error. This error can be intercepted and handled by overriding the `doesNotUnderstand:` method in a subclass of Object to provide an alternative way of handling the error【4:0†source】.

In the case where the `doesNotUnderstand:` method is overridden by a subclass of Object in the lookup path, it allows for dynamic behavior where an object can fall back to an alternative strategy for responding to a message that it does not understand【4:5†source】.

To address the issue of receiving a `MessageNotUnderstood` error like in the scenario described, one approach is to intercept the error and dynamically load or generate the missing methods. ,To invoke shell commands from Squeak and Pharo, you do not directly invoke methods but rather send messages. In Pharo, everything is based on sending messages to objects, and the receiver of the message dynamically selects the method to execute in response. This means that the responsibility of selecting the method lies with the receiver of the message, not the client sending the message【4:2†source】.

In Pharo, certain objects can only perform tasks by invoking virtual machine primitives. These primitives are invoked with specific syntax, and if a primitive fails, Pharo code can be included in the method to be executed as a fallback. For example, memory allocation, bit manipulation, arithmetic operations, and array access are implemented as primitives in Pharo【4:0†source】.

Pharo does not have a direct equivalent to the system() function in Unix languages for running external shell commands. Instead of invoking shell commands directly, Pharo focuses on sending messages to objects for execution, following the object-oriented paradigm of the language【4:8†source】.

Therefore, in Pharo, the approach to executing external commands is different from directly invoking shell commands as in Unix languages. The emphasis is on sending messages to objects and allowing the receiver to dynamically select the appropriate method for execution, rather than invoking shell commands explicitly【4:2†source】.",204,,,,,0.5,0.7186709382472424,5,"['Certain objects can only get work done by invoking virtual machine primitives .', 'Ok , so nearly everything in Pharo happens by sending messages .', 'Pharo is based on the concept of sending messages to objects .', 'A method that invokes such a primitive may also include Pharo code , which will be executed only if the primitive fails .', 'It implies that this is not the responsibility of the client to select the method to be executed , it is the one of the receiver of the message .']","['In Pharo , certain objects can only perform tasks by invoking virtual machine primitives .', 'In Pharo , everything is based on sending messages to objects , and the receiver of the message dynamically selects the method to execute in response .', 'Instead of invoking shell commands directly , Pharo focuses on sending messages to objects for execution , following the object - oriented paradigm of the language[(4:8+source )]  .', 'These primitives are invoked with specific syntax , and if a primitive fails , Pharo code can be included in the method to be executed as a fallback .', 'This means that the responsibility of selecting the method lies with the receiver of the message , not the client sending the message[(4:2+source )]  .']",156,3439,273,,,pharo,gpt-3.5-turbo

,In Python, the `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement is used to check whether the current script is being run directly by the Python interpreter, or if it is being imported as a module into another script.
When a Python script is run, the special variable `__name__` is set to `""__main__""`. This allows you to differentiate between when the script is being run directly or being imported.

Including the `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement is useful when you have code in your script that you only want to run when the script is executed directly, and not when it is imported as a module. This is commonly used for defining functions, classes, or variables that you want to be available for importing, but you don't want them to be executed when the script is imported.
For example, consider the following script:

In this script, the `say_hello()` function will only be executed when the script is run directly. If the script is imported into another script, the `say_hello()` function will be available for use, but it will not be automatically executed.

Explanation: the methods based on + (including the implied use in sum) are, of necessity, O(L**2) when there are L sublists -- as the intermediate result list keeps getting longer, at each step a new intermediate result list object gets allocated, and all the items in the previous intermediate result must be copied over (as well as a few new ones added at the end). So, for simplicity and without actual loss of generality, say you have L sublists of M items each: the first M items are copied back and forth L-1 times, the second M items L-2 times, and so on
column2: total number of copies is M times the sum of x for x from 1 to L excluded, i.e., M * (L**2)/2.

,

To understand what yield does, you must understand what generators are. And before you can understand generators, you must understand iterables.
When you create a list, you can read its items one by one. Reading its items one by one is called iteration:
mylist is an iterable. When you use a list comprehension, you create a list, and so an iterable:
These iterables are handy because you can read them as much as you wish, but you store all the values in memory and this is not always what you want when you have a lot of values.
Generators are iterators, a kind of iterable you can only iterate over once. Generators do not store all the values in memory, they generate the values on the fly:

It is just the same except you used () instead of []. BUT, you cannot perform for i in mygenerator a second time since generators can only be used once: they calculate 0, then forget about it and calculate 1, and end after calculating 4, one by one.
yield is a keyword that is used like return, except the function will return a generator.
Here it's a useless example, but it's handy when you know your function will return a huge set of values that you will only need to read once.

To master yield, you must understand that when you call the function, the code you have written in the function body does not run. The function only returns the generator object, this is a bit tricky.
Then, your code will continue from where it left off each time for uses the generator.

The first time the for calls the generator object created from your function, it will run the code in your function from the beginning until it hits yield, then it'll return the first value of the loop. Then, each subsequent call will run another iteration of the loop you have written in the function and return the next value. This will continue until the generator is considered empty, which happens when the function runs without hitting yield. That can be because the loop has come to an end, or because you no longer satisfy an "

• The extend() method is a list object method that expects an iterable and adds its values to the list.
Usually, we pass a list to it:
But in your code, it gets a generator, which is good because:

And it works because Python does not care if the argument of a method is a list or not. Python expects iterables so it will work with strings, lists, tuples, and generators! This is called duck typing and is one of the reasons why Python is so cool. But this is another story, for another question...

In the provided code snippet, the `_get_child_candidates` method is a generator function. When this method is called, it yields the `_leftchild` and `_rightchild` nodes based on certain conditions. Each time the generator function is called, it yields one element at a time. The subsequent calls to the generator function will continue to yield elements until there are no more values to yield. In this case, the subsequent calls will stop when there are no more child nodes that meet the specified conditions.

Additionally, you can use tuples as keys in dictionaries, as tuples are immutable and can be used as keys. For instance, in a telephone directory mapping last-name, first-name pairs to telephone numbers, you can use tuple assignment to traverse the dictionary and access the values associated with each key (Think Python, p. 120).

It's important to note that dictionaries in Python require keys to be hashable, which means they must be immutable. Mutable objects like lists cannot be used as keys in dictionaries. To find the directory where the Python file being executed is located, you can use os.path.abspath('filename'). This function returns the absolute path to the file, regardless of the current directory (Think Python, p. 139). For instance, os.path.abspath('memo.txt') will return '/home/dinsdale/memo.txt'.

A path that begins with '/' is an absolute path and does not depend on the current directory, while a simple filename like 'memo.txt' is a relative path that relates to the current directory (Think Python, p. 139). If you need to work with files and directories, the os module provides functions like os.path.exists, os.path.isdir, and os.path.isfile to check for file existence, directory status, and file type, respectively (Think Python, p. 140).

Additionally, you can use os.listdir(dirname) to list the files and directories in a given directory. To recursively walk through a directory and its subdirectories, you can create a function like the provided 'walk' function that prints the names of all files (Think Python, p. 140). This function uses os.path.join to create complete paths and os.path.isfile to identify files.

Similarly, the `float` function converts integers and strings to floating-point numbers (Think Python, pp. 17-18). For instance, `float(32)` would return `32.0`, and `float('3.14159')` would return `3.14159`.

If you expect user input to be an integer, you can convert the input to an integer using `int(input())` (Think Python, pp. 45-46). However, if the user types something other than a string of digits, you will encounter an error.

It's important to note that when dealing with strings that look like numbers, such as '2' or '42.0', they are still considered strings in Python (Think Python, p. 4). To convert these strings to integers or floats, you can use the `int` or `float` functions accordingly.

In Python, you can't perform mathematical operations on strings, except for concatenation using the `+` operator and repetition using the `*` operator (Think Python, p. 12). For example, `'Hello, ' + 'World!'` would result in `'Hello, World!'`.

column1: It's boilerplate code that protects users from accidentally invoking the script when they didn't intend to. Here are some common problems when the guard is omitted from a script:

• If you import the guardless script in another script (e.g. import my_script_without_a_name_eq_main_guard), then the latter script will trigger the former to run at import time and using the second script's command line arguments. This is almost always a mistake.

• If you have a custom class in the guardless script and save it to a pickle file, then unpickling it in another script will trigger an import of the guardless script, with the same problems outlined in the previous bullet.
To better understand why and how this matters, we need to take a step back to understand how Python initializes scripts and how this interacts with its module import mechanism.

column2: if the directory is created between the os.path.exists and the os.makedirs calls, the os.makedirs will fail with an OSError. Unfortunately, blanket-catching OSError and continuing is not foolproof, as it will ignore a failure to create the directory due to other factors, such as insufficient permissions, full disk, etc.

column1: One option would be to trap the OSError and examine the embedded error code (see Is there a cross-platform way of getting information from Python’s OSError):
Alternatively, there could be a second os.path.exists, but suppose another created the directory after the first check, then removed it before the second one &ndash

Depending on the application, the danger of concurrent operations may be more or less than the danger posed by other factors such as file permissions. The developer would have to know more about the particular application being developed and its expected environment before choosing an implementation.
Modern versions of Python improve this code quite a bit, both by exposing FileExistsError (in 3.3+)...
...and by allowing a keyword argument to os.makedirs called exist_ok (in 3.2+).

column1: It is my understanding (as well as the understanding of the creator of the language) that the intended usage for dict(**y) is for creating dictionaries for readability purposes, e.g.:

Despite what Guido says, dict(x, **y) is in line with the dict specification, which btw. works for both Python 2 and 3. The fact that this only works for string keys is a direct consequence of how keyword parameters work and not a short-coming of dict. Nor is using the ** operator in this place an abuse of the mechanism, in fact, ** was designed precisely to pass dictionaries as keywords.

Again, it doesn't work for 3 when keys are not strings. The implicit calling contract is that namespaces take ordinary dictionaries, while users must only pass keyword arguments that are strings. All other callables enforced it. dict broke this consistency in Python 2:
TypeError: foo() keywords must be strings
This inconsistency was bad given other implementations of Python (PyPy, Jython, IronPython). Thus it was fixed in Python 3, as this usage could be a breaking change.
I submit to you that it is malicious incompetence to intentionally write code that only works in one version of a language or that only works given certain arbitrary constraints.
dict(x.items() + y.items()) is still the most readable solution for Python 2. Readability counts.
