RAG,"What does the ""yield"" keyword do in Python?","

What functionality does the yield keyword in Python provide?
For example, I'm trying to understand this code1:
What happens when the method _get_child_candidates is called?
Is a list returned? A single element? Is it called again? When will subsequent calls stop?
1. This piece of code was written by Jochen Schulz (jrschulz), who made a great Python library for metric spaces. This is the link to the complete source: Module mspace.

,

To understand what yield does, you must understand what generators are. And before you can understand generators, you must understand iterables.
When you create a list, you can read its items one by one. Reading its items one by one is called iteration:
mylist is an iterable. When you use a list comprehension, you create a list, and so an iterable:
These iterables are handy because you can read them as much as you wish, but you store all the values in memory and this is not always what you want when you have a lot of values.
Generators are iterators, a kind of iterable you can only iterate over once. Generators do not store all the values in memory, they generate the values on the fly:

It is just the same except you used () instead of []. BUT, you cannot perform for i in mygenerator a second time since generators can only be used once: they calculate 0, then forget about it and calculate 1, and end after calculating 4, one by one.
yield is a keyword that is used like return, except the function will return a generator.
Here it's a useless example, but it's handy when you know your function will return a huge set of values that you will only need to read once.

To master yield, you must understand that when you call the function, the code you have written in the function body does not run. The function only returns the generator object, this is a bit tricky.
Then, your code will continue from where it left off each time for uses the generator.

The first time the for calls the generator object created from your function, it will run the code in your function from the beginning until it hits yield, then it'll return the first value of the loop. Then, each subsequent call will run another iteration of the loop you have written in the function and return the next value. This will continue until the generator is considered empty, which happens when the function runs without hitting yield. That can be because the loop has come to an end, or because you no longer satisfy an "
<pre><code># Here you create the method of the node object that will return the generator
# Here is the code that will be called each time you use the generator object:
# If there is still a child of the node object on its left
# AND if the distance is ok, return the next child
# If there is still a child of the node object on its right
# AND if the distance is ok, return the next child
# If the function arrives here, the generator will be considered empty
# There are no more than two values: the left and the right children
<pre><code># Create an empty list and a list with the current object reference
# Loop on candidates (they contain only one element at the beginning)
# Get the last candidate and remove it from the list
# Get the distance between obj and the candidate
# If the distance is ok, then you can fill in the result
# Add the children of the candidate to the candidate's list
# so the loop will keep running until it has looked
# at all the children of the children of the children, etc. of the candidate

• The loop iterates on a list, but the list expands while the loop is being iterated. It's a concise way to go through all these nested data even if it's a bit dangerous since you can end up with an infinite loop. In this case, candidates.extend(node._get_child_candidates(distance, min_dist, max_dist)) exhausts all the values of the generator, but while keeps creating new generator objects which will produce different values from the previous ones since it's not applied on the same node.
• The extend() method is a list object method that expects an iterable and adds its values to the list.
Usually, we pass a list to it:
But in your code, it gets a generator, which is good because:
• You don't need to read the values twice.
• You may have a lot of children and you don't want them all stored in memory.

And it works because Python does not care if the argument of a method is a list or not. Python expects iterables so it will work with strings, lists, tuples, and generators! This is called duck typing and is one of the reasons why Python is so cool. But this is another story, for another question...

,The ""yield"" keyword in Python is used to create a generator function, which allows the function to return a value without terminating. When a function contains the ""yield"" keyword, it becomes a generator function, and calling this function does not return a list or a single element immediately. Instead, it returns a generator object that can be iterated over to retrieve the values one by one (p. 183).

In the provided code snippet, the method `_get_child_candidates` contains the `yield` keyword, which means that when this method is called, it returns a generator object that yields the `_leftchild` and `_rightchild` elements based on certain conditions (p. 183). The caller function then iterates over this generator object to retrieve the values one by one and appends them to the `result` list (p. 183).

Subsequent calls to the `_get_child_candidates` method will continue to yield values based on the conditions specified in the function until all elements have been processed. The generator object created by the `yield` keyword allows for lazy evaluation, meaning that values are generated only when needed, making it memory-efficient for large datasets (p. 183).",182,0.0,0.0,0.626643269987986,,0.3333333333333333,0.5488431429143327,1,['The result is a generator object that knows how to iterate through a sequence of values .'],"['Instead , it returns a generator object that can be iterated over to retrieve the values one by one ( p. 183 ) .']",436,1156,241,0.0,[],python,gpt-3.5-turbo

,The most ""pythonic"" way to find an item in a list is by using the `in` operator, as shown in the code snippet below (p. 104):

The `in` operator uses different algorithms for lists and dictionaries. For lists, it searches the elements of the list in order, which means the search time increases linearly as the list gets longer (p. 104). On the other hand, Python dictionaries use a hashtable data structure, allowing the `in` operator to take about the same amount of time regardless of the number of items in the dictionary (p. 104).

If you need to perform a reverse lookup in a dictionary, where you have a value and want to find the corresponding key, you can use a function like `reverse_lookup(d, v)` (pp. 106 - 107). This function iterates through the dictionary to find the first key that maps to the given value. However, reverse lookups are slower than forward lookups, so they should be used judiciously to avoid performance issues (p. 107).

Additionally, if you want to check whether a word is in a list efficiently, especially if the list is sorted, you can use a bisection search (binary search) algorithm. This algorithm is similar to looking up a word in a dictionary, where you start in the middle and iteratively narrow down the search space by checking if the word is before or after the middle point (p. 101).

,To parse a string to a float or int in Python, you can use the built-in functions `float()` and `int()`. The `int()` function converts a value to an integer, chopping off the fraction part if it's a floating-point value (Think Python, pp. 17 - 18). For example, `int('32')` would result in `32`. Similarly, the `float()` function converts integers and strings to floating-point numbers without rounding off (Think Python, pp. 17 - 18). For instance, `float('3.14159')` would yield `3.14159`.

Integers, strings, and floating-point numbers belong to different types in Python. Integers represent whole numbers, floating-point numbers represent numbers with fractional parts, and strings represent sequences of characters (Think Python, p. 4). The type of a value can be determined using the `type()` function in Python. For example, `type('2')` and `type('42.0')` would both return `<class 'str'>`, indicating that they are strings (Think Python, p. 4).

When parsing a string to an integer using `int()`, if the string contains non-numeric characters, a `ValueError` will be raised (Think Python, pp. 45 - 46). It's important to handle such errors appropriately in your code. Additionally, Python does not allow commas in large integers like `1,000,000`, interpreting them as a sequence of integers instead (Think Python, p. 4).

In summary, to convert a string to a float or int in Python, use the `float()` or `int()` functions, respectively. Ensure that the string contains valid numeric characters to avoid errors, and be aware of the differences in types between integers, strings, and floating-point numbers in Python.",247,0.0,0.0,0.0,,0.2222222222222222,0.6394996425076348,2,"[""The int function takes any value and converts it to an integer , if it can , or complains otherwise : > > > int('32 ' ) 32 > > > int('Hello ' ) ValueError : invalid literal for int ( ): Hello int can convert floating - point values to integers , but it does n't round off

,The statement `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` in Python is used to check if the current script is being run directly or if it is being imported as a module. When a Python script is run, the built-in variable `__name__` is set to `__main__`, indicating that the script is the main program being executed. In this case, any code within the `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` block will run. If the script is imported as a module into another script, the value of `__name__` will be the name of the module instead of `__main__`, and the code within the block will not run (Think Python, p. 144).

Including the `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement is important because it allows you to write code that can be both imported as a module and run as a standalone script. This way, you can define functions and classes in your script that can be reused in other scripts by importing the module, while also being able to run the script independently to test or execute specific functionality.
For example, consider the following code snippet:
When this script is run directly, it will print ""Hello, World!"" to the console. However, if this script is imported as a module into another script, the print statement will not be executed.

In conclusion, using the `if __name__ == ""__main__"":` statement in Python allows for modular and reusable code by distinguishing between when a script is run directly or imported as a module. This helps in organizing and structuring Python programs effectively (Think Python, p. 144).",261,0.0,0.0,0.5934789155493638,,0.1666666666666666,0.5946228971784235,1,"[""If the program is running as a script , _ _ name _ _ has the value ' _ _""]","['If the script is imported as a module into another script , the value of ` _ _ name _ _ ` will be the name of the module instead of ` _ _']",302,2612,355,4.0,"['What is the value of __name__ when wc is being run as a script?', ""What should you use Python's script mode for?"", 'What does the Python interpreter do when in script mode?', 'Why is it important to ensure that the program Python is trying to run is the one you are editing?']",python,gpt-3.5-turbo

,To get the current time in Python, you can use the time module, which provides a function that returns the current Greenwich Mean Time in ""the epoch"" (Think Python, p. 47). The epoch is an arbitrary time used as a reference point, with UNIX systems using 1 January 1970 as the epoch. By importing the time module and calling the time function, you can retrieve the current time in seconds since the epoch.

To convert the current time to a time of day in hours, minutes, seconds, and the number of days since the epoch, you can write a script that reads the current time and performs the necessary calculations (Think Python, p. 48). Additionally, the datetime module in Python provides time objects similar to the Time objects discussed in the textbook, with a rich set of methods and operators available (Think Python, p. 160).

When calling the print_time function, you need to pass a Time object as an argument, either using function syntax or method syntax (Think Python, p. 162). The program using the datetime module can perform tasks such as getting the current date and printing the day of the week, calculating a user's age and the time until their next birthday, or determining a ""Double Day"" for two people born on different days (Think Python, p. 160).

In Python, when an operator is used with Time objects, the computation is dispatched to different methods based on the type of the arguments (Think Python, p. 166). This process, known as operator overloading, allows for custom behavior of operators with programmer-defined types. RAG+CoI,How do I make a flat list out of a list of lists?,"

I have a list of lists like
How can I flatten it to get [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9]?
If your list of lists comes from a nested list comprehension, the problem can be solved more simply/directly by fixing the comprehension
column2: please see How can I get a flat result from a list comprehension instead of a nested list?.

column1: The most popular solutions here generally only flatten one "
column3: of the nested list. See Flatten an irregular (arbitrarily nested) list of lists for solutions that completely flatten a deeply nested structure (recursively, in general).

column1:
,

A list of lists named xss can be flattened using a nested list comprehension:
return [x for xs in xss for x in xs]
As evidence, using the timeit module in the standard library, we see:
<pre class=""lang-bash prettyprint-override""><code>$ python -mtimeit -s'xss=[[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7],[8,9]]*99' '[x for xs in xss for x in xs]'
10000 loops, best of 3: 143 usec per loop
1000 loops, best of 3: 969 usec per loop
$ python -mtimeit -s'xss=[[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7],[8,9]]*99' 'reduce(lambda xs, ys: xs + ys, xss)'
1000 loops, best of 3: 1.1 msec per loop

Explanation: the methods based on + (including the implied use in sum) are, of necessity, O(L**2) when there are L sublists -- as the intermediate result list keeps getting longer, at each step a new intermediate result list object gets allocated, and all the items in the previous intermediate result must be copied over (as well as a few new ones added at the end). So, for simplicity and without actual loss of generality, say you have L sublists of M items each: the first M items are copied back and forth L-1 times, the second M items L-2 times, and so on
column2: total number of copies is M times the sum of x for x from 1 to L excluded, i.e., M * (L**2)/2.

column1: The list comprehension just generates one list, once, and copies each item over (from its original place of residence to the result list) also exactly once.

,To flatten a list of lists, you can use list comprehension. How do I create or use a global variable inside a function?
How do I use a global variable that was defined in one function inside other functions?

Failing to use the global keyword where appropriate often causes UnboundLocalError. The precise rules for this are explained at UnboundLocalError on local variable when reassigned after first use. Generally, please close other questions as a duplicate of that question when an explanation is sought, and this question when someone simply needs to know the global keyword.

,

You can use a global variable within other functions by declaring it as global within each function that assigns a value to it:
global globvar    # Needed to modify global copy of globvar
print(globvar)     # No need for global declaration to read value of globvar

Since it's unclear whether globvar = 1 is creating a local variable or changing a global variable, Python defaults to creating a local variable, and makes you explicitly choose the other behavior with the global keyword.
See other answers if you want to share a global variable across modules.

,To use a global variable in a function in Python, you need to declare the variable as global within the function before using it. This ensures that the function knows to reference the global variable rather than creating a new local variable with the same name (Think Python, pp. 110).

For example, if you have a global variable `been_called` and want to update it inside a function, you should declare it as global within the function before reassigning its value. Otherwise, the function will create a new local variable with the same name, which won't affect the global variable (Think Python, pp. 110).

Additionally, global variables in Python can be useful for maintaining values across different function calls. They persist from one function call to the next and can be accessed from any function, unlike local variables that disappear when the function ends (Think Python, pp. 110).

Remember that if a global variable refers to a mutable value like a list or dictionary, you can modify the value without declaring the variable as global. However, if you want to reassign the variable itself, you must declare it as global within the function (Think Python, pp. 110-111).

In summary, to use a global variable in a function in Python, declare the variable as global within the function before using or updating its value. How do I create a directory at a given path, and also create any missing parent directories along that path? For example, the Bash command mkdir -p /path/to/nested/directory does this.
For older versions of Python, I see two answers with good qualities, each with a small flaw, so I will give my take on it:
As noted in comments and elsewhere, there's a race condition &ndash
column2: if the directory is created between the os.path.exists and the os.makedirs calls, the os.makedirs will fail with an OSError. Unfortunately, blanket-catching OSError and continuing is not foolproof, as it will ignore a failure to create the directory due to other factors, such as insufficient permissions, full disk, etc.

column1: One option would be to trap the OSError and examine the embedded error code (see Is there a cross-platform way of getting information from Python’s OSError):
Alternatively, there could be a second os.path.exists, but suppose another created the directory after the first check, then removed it before the second one &ndash

Depending on the application, the danger of concurrent operations may be more or less than the danger posed by other factors such as file permissions. The developer would have to know more about the particular application being developed and its expected environment before choosing an implementation.
Modern versions of Python improve this code quite a bit, both by exposing FileExistsError (in 3.3+)...
...and by allowing a keyword argument to os.makedirs called exist_ok (in 3.2+).
<pre><code>os.makedirs(""path/to/directory"", exist_ok=True)  # succeeds even if directory exists.

,To create a directory at a given path and also create any missing parent directories along that path, you can use the `os.makedirs()` function in Python. This function creates a directory and any missing parent directories recursively on the specified path (Think Python, p. 140).

The `os.makedirs()` function will create the directory `nested` inside the directory `to`, and the directory `to` inside the directory `path`, if they do not already exist. This ensures that the entire directory structure specified in the path is created.

column1: It is my understanding (as well as the understanding of the creator of the language) that the intended usage for dict(**y) is for creating dictionaries for readability purposes, e.g.:

Despite what Guido says, dict(x, **y) is in line with the dict specification, which btw. works for both Python 2 and 3. The fact that this only works for string keys is a direct consequence of how keyword parameters work and not a short-coming of dict. Nor is using the ** operator in this place an abuse of the mechanism, in fact, ** was designed precisely to pass dictionaries as keywords.

Again, it doesn't work for 3 when keys are not strings. The implicit calling contract is that namespaces take ordinary dictionaries, while users must only pass keyword arguments that are strings. All other callables enforced it. dict broke this consistency in Python 2:
TypeError: foo() keywords must be strings
This inconsistency was bad given other implementations of Python (PyPy, Jython, IronPython). Thus it was fixed in Python 3, as this usage could be a breaking change.
I submit to you that it is malicious incompetence to intentionally write code that only works in one version of a language or that only works given certain arbitrary constraints.
dict(x.items() + y.items()) is still the most readable solution for Python 2. Readability counts.

To understand what yield does, you must understand what generators are. And before you can understand generators, you must understand iterables.
When you create a list, you can read its items one by one. Reading its items one by one is called iteration:
mylist is an iterable. When you use a list comprehension, you create a list, and so an iterable:
These iterables are handy because you can read them as much as you wish, but you store all the values in memory and this is not always what you want when you have a lot of values.
Generators are iterators, a kind of iterable you can only iterate over once. Generators do not store all the values in memory, they generate the values on the fly:

It is just the same except you used () instead of []. BUT, you cannot perform for i in mygenerator a second time since generators can only be used once: they calculate 0, then forget about it and calculate 1, and end after calculating 4, one by one.
yield is a keyword that is used like return, except the function will return a generator.
Here it's a useless example, but it's handy when you know your function will return a huge set of values that you will only need to read once.

To master yield, you must understand that when you call the function, the code you have written in the function body does not run. The function only returns the generator object, this is a bit tricky.
Then, your code will continue from where it left off each time for uses the generator.

The first time the for calls the generator object created from your function, it will run the code in your function from the beginning until it hits yield, then it'll return the first value of the loop. Then, each subsequent call will run another iteration of the loop you have written in the function and return the next value. This will continue until the generator is considered empty, which happens when the function runs without hitting yield. That can be because the loop has come to an end, or because you no longer satisfy an "

In this case, candidates.extend(node._get_child_candidates(distance, min_dist, max_dist)) exhausts all the values of the generator, but while keeps creating new generator objects which will produce different values from the previous ones since it's not applied on the same node.

But in your code, it gets a generator, which is good because:

This method is efficient for lists as it uses different algorithms for lists and dictionaries. For lists, it searches the elements of the list in order, and as the list gets longer, the search time increases in direct proportion (p. 104). However, Python dictionaries use a hashtable data structure, making the `in` operator take about the same amount of time regardless of the number of items in the dictionary (p. 104).

Additionally, to check whether a word is in a list, a bisection search (binary search) can be used to speed up the process, especially if the words are in alphabetical order (p. 101). This method involves starting in the middle of the list and checking whether the word being searched for comes before or after the word in the middle, effectively cutting the remaining search space in half.

In the context of dictionaries, the `in` operator can also be used to check if a value exists in a dictionary's values (p. 104). To see whether something appears as a value in a dictionary, the `values` method can be used to return a collection of values, which can then be checked using the `in` operator.

column1: It's boilerplate code that protects users from accidentally invoking the script when they didn't intend to. Here are some common problems when the guard is omitted from a script:

• If you import the guardless script in another script (e.g. import my_script_without_a_name_eq_main_guard), then the latter script will trigger the former to run at import time and using the second script's command line arguments. This is almost always a mistake.

• If you have a custom class in the guardless script and save it to a pickle file, then unpickling it in another script will trigger an import of the guardless script, with the same problems outlined in the previous bullet.
To better understand why and how this matters, we need to take a step back to understand how Python initializes scripts and how this interacts with its module import mechanism.
Whenever the Python interpreter reads a source file, it does two things:

global globvar    # Needed to modify global copy of globvar

Python calls this concept ""slicing"" and it works on more than just strings. Take a look here for a comprehensive introduction.
,To get a substring of a string in Python, you can use string slicing. Here's how it works based on the information from ""Think Python"":

1. If you want to get a new string from the third character to the end of the string, you can use slicing. For example, to get a substring starting from the third character of a string `myString` to the end, you can use `myString[2:]`【4:0†source】.

2. If you omit the first part of the slice (before the colon), the slice starts at the beginning of the string. For instance, `fruit[:3]` would return the substring from the start up to the third character of the string 'banana', which is 'ban'【4:0†source】.

3. Omitting the second part of the slice means the slice goes to the end of the string. For example, `fruit[3:]` would return the substring from the fourth character to the end of the string 'banana', which is 'ana'【4:0†source】.
4. If you omit both parts of the slice, the result is a copy of the whole string. For instance, `t[:]` would return a copy of the entire list `t`【4:1†source】.

In summary, in Python, omitting the first index in a slice starts it from the beginning of the string, and omitting the second index extends it to the end of the string. Nor is using the ** operator in this place an abuse of the mechanism, in fact, ** was designed precisely to pass dictionaries as keywords.

1. This piece of code was written by Jochen Schulz (jrschulz), who made a great Python library for metric spaces. This is the link to the complete source: Module mspace.

,

To understand what yield does, you must understand what generators are. And before you can understand generators, you must understand iterables.
When you create a list, you can read its items one by one. Reading its items one by one is called iteration:
mylist is an iterable. When you use a list comprehension, you create a list, and so an iterable:
These iterables are handy because you can read them as much as you wish, but you store all the values in memory and this is not always what you want when you have a lot of values.
Generators are iterators, a kind of iterable you can only iterate over once. Generators do not store all the values in memory, they generate the values on the fly:

It is just the same except you used () instead of []. BUT, you cannot perform for i in mygenerator a second time since generators can only be used once: they calculate 0, then forget about it and calculate 1, and end after calculating 4, one by one.
yield is a keyword that is used like return, except the function will return a generator.
Here it's a useless example, but it's handy when you know your function will return a huge set of values that you will only need to read once.

To master yield, you must understand that when you call the function, the code you have written in the function body does not run. The function only returns the generator object, this is a bit tricky.
Then, your code will continue from where it left off each time for uses the generator.

The first time the for calls the generator object created from your function, it will run the code in your function from the beginning until it hits yield, then it'll return the first value of the loop. Then, each subsequent call will run another iteration of the loop you have written in the function and return the next value. This will continue until the generator is considered empty, which happens when the function runs without hitting yield. That can be because the loop has come to an end, or because you no longer satisfy an "
<pre><code># Here you create the method of the node object that will return the generator
# Here is the code that will be called each time you use the generator object:
# If there is still a child of the node object on its left
# AND if the distance is ok, return the next child
# If there is still a child of the node object on its right
# AND if the distance is ok, return the next child
# If the function arrives here, the generator will be considered empty
# There are no more than two values: the left and the right children
<pre><code># Create an empty list and a list with the current object reference
# Loop on candidates (they contain only one element at the beginning)
# Get the last candidate and remove it from the list
# Get the distance between obj and the candidate
# If the distance is ok, then you can fill in the result
# Add the children of the candidate to the candidate's list
# so the loop will keep running until it has looked
# at all the children of the children of the children, etc. of the candidate

• The loop iterates on a list, but the list expands while the loop is being iterated. It's a concise way to go through all these nested data even if it's a bit dangerous since you can end up with an infinite loop. In this case, candidates.extend(node._get_child_candidates(distance, min_dist, max_dist)) exhausts all the values of the generator, but while keeps creating new generator objects which will produce different values from the previous ones since it's not applied on the same node.
• The extend() method is a list object method that expects an iterable and adds its values to the list.
Usually, we pass a list to it:
But in your code, it gets a generator, which is good because:
• You don't need to read the values twice.
• You may have a lot of children and you don't want them all stored in memory.

And it works because Python does not care if the argument of a method is a list or not. Python expects iterables so it will work with strings, lists, tuples, and generators! This is called duck typing and is one of the reasons why Python is so cool. But this is another story, for another question...
